channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: If you can think it...
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
“Future thinking” is a term that’s often employed these days in policy circles, think tanks, and research laboratories. It’s also used a lot in the technology world, when imagining new tools for emergent needs – or needs that have not yet been felt. Sometimes referred to as “futures thinking” (in the plural), it can take a systematic and rule-based approach to construct scenarios and build trajectories to achieve them or change them. It requires imagination and the ability to speculate, but also the skill of seeing pathways and how to achieve the desired future, or in some cases, how to avoid a negative scenario that seems to be looming. Climate change activists and environmentalists for instance try to think about what would happen if we continued destroying our habitat at our current rate, and then work backwards to figure out how we might avoid or better manage, a catastrophe.

But wouldn’t you agree that teaching is – or should be – a project of making the future? Day after day in our classrooms, we are working with young minds so that they can be ready for the world when they step out into it. Even when we are dealing with subjects that explain the world as it is, or as it has come to be, the point is to equip them with the understanding and skills that will help them occupy that world and remake it in better ways. The classroom is, of course, embedded within a complex and ever-changing ecology, and thinking about the future cannot disregard the problems of our present. Ultimately, it’s a careful combination of recognizing where we are (pragmatism), seeing where we want to go (idealism), and thinking about how we get there, given our strengths and challenges (strategy). So, for us as teachers, it involves staying in touch with what is happening in our respective fields and in society, and making our teaching relevant in ways that can serve children both the present and the future. Yes, there is exam-directed work, but there also needs to be skilling and mindset building for resilience and adaptability. I find that it’s useful, now and then, to stop what we are doing and just loosen our minds with what is often called “blue sky thinking”. Ask yourself, what is the kind of future I want? Allow yourself to fantasize about this perfect world, indulging in painting the smallest details. Then pull back and think about what needs to be done in the present to achieve at least some part of it. Is there something here that can trickle down to what and how you teach?

But for now: Happy Teacher’s Day!
item.excerpt:encoded: “Future thinking” is a term that’s often employed these days in policy circles, think tanks, and research laboratories. It’s also used a lot in the technology world, when imagining new tools for emergent needs – or needs that have not yet been felt.

item.title: The silent warriors: how beneficial soil fungi protect plants
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Andleeb Zehra

Soil is not just a growing medium for plants, it is a dynamic ecosystem teeming with microorganisms that influence plant health. Among these, beneficial soil fungi play a crucial role in plant defense, acting as natural protectors against harmful pathogens. These fungi enhance plant resilience, improve soil health, reduce dependency on chemical pesticides, and contribute to sustainable agriculture.

In recent years, with the increasing challenges of pesticide resistance, environmental degradation, and soil depletion, there has been a growing interest in biological alternatives to chemical fungicides. Beneficial soil fungi, particularly Trichoderma, have gained attention for their ability to suppress harmful pathogens, enhance plant immunity, and promote healthier crop growth. This article explores the role of beneficial soil fungi in plant protection, their mechanisms of action, advantages over chemical fungicides, and future potential in agriculture.

Understanding beneficial soil fungi

Soil fungi are highly diverse and can be broadly classified into decomposers, mutualistic fungi, and pathogenic fungi. While some fungi cause plant diseases, others serve as defenders by suppressing harmful microbes and improving soil health.

One of the most well-known beneficial fungi is Trichoderma, which acts as a natural biocontrol agent (Fig.1). It has been widely studied for its ability to out-compete plant pathogens, stimulate plant immunity, and improve root development.

Mechanisms of plant protection

Beneficial fungi protect plants through multiple mechanisms, including competition for resources, direct antagonism, stimulation of plant immunity, and soil health improvement.

Competition for resources: Trichoderma and other beneficial fungi out-compete harmful pathogens like Fusarium for essential nutrients, water, and space in the rhizosphere. This prevents pathogenic fungi from colonizing plant roots and therefore reduce infection risks.

Mycoparasitism (direct attack on pathogens): Some fungi act as natural predators of harmful microbes. Trichoderma wraps around pathogenic fungi like Fusarium and secretes enzymes that break down their cell walls, effectively neutralizing them (Fig.2).

Certain beneficial fungi enhance a plant’s own defense system, similar to how vaccines prepare the immune system in humans. They trigger biochemical responses in plants, enabling them to resist future infections more effectively. This natural immunity boost reduces dependency on chemical fungicides.

Soil health improvement

Beneficial fungi play a crucial role in soil formation and nutrient cycling. They decompose organic matter, releasing essential nutrients that support plant growth. They improve soil structure, making it more aerated and capable of retaining moisture, which is beneficial for plant roots.

Sustainable alternatives to chemical pesticides

Excessive use of chemical fungicides has led to:

Environmental pollution– Contaminates water bodies and reduces biodiversity.

Soil degradation– Kills beneficial microbes, leading to poor soil fertility.

Resistant pathogens – Overuse of chemicals has led to pathogens developing resistance, reducing fungicide effectiveness.

Beneficial fungi offer a sustainable alternative by providing long-term plant protection without environmental harm. Self-sustaining populations remain active in the soil over multiple growing seasons. Biofungicides like Root Shield, derived from Trichoderma, are now gaining popularity in modern agriculture.

Key fact: Studies show that using Trichoderma-based biofungicides reduces fungal infections by up to 60% while improving crop yields.

Challenges and future prospects

Despite their many advantages, the widespread adoption of beneficial fungi in agriculture faces challenges:

Lack of awareness: Many farmers lack knowledge about fungal biocontrol methods and continue to rely on chemical fungicides.

Availability and cost: Biofungicides are not always affordable or widely available, especially to small-scale farmers.

Environmental factors: The effectiveness of beneficial fungi depends on soil conditions, temperature, and microbial competition.

Future prospects:

Ongoing research and awareness campaigns are addressing these challenges. Advances in biotechnology are improving the efficacy and availability of fungal-based solutions. Government policies and subsidies for biofungicides can help encourage farmers to adopt sustainable alternatives.

Conclusion

Beneficial soil fungi are silent warriors in modern agriculture, offering natural plant protection without the environmental harm associated with chemical pesticides. By understanding their mechanisms of action and potential applications, farmers can adopt biological control methods to improve crop resilience, reduce chemical dependency, and promote soil health.

Key takeaways

Trichoderma and other beneficial fungi act as natural biocontrol agents, suppressing harmful pathogens. These fungi enhance plant immunity, improve soil fertility, and promote long-term agricultural sustainability. Biofungicides derived from beneficial fungi reduce dependency on chemical pesticides, lowering environmental pollution. Despite challenges like cost, awareness, and environmental variability, advances in research are expanding the use of fungal-based plant protection methods.

With continued research, policy support, and farmer education, beneficial soil fungi can revolutionize plant disease management and ensure a healthier, more sustainable future for agriculture.

Classroom Connection:

Textbook link:
This topic connects with chapters such as Microorganisms: Friend and Foe (Class 8, NCERT) and Biological Classification / Microbes in Human Welfare (Class 11–12, NCERT/CBSE). These chapters discuss how microbes influence agriculture, soil health, and plant growth.

Suggested teacher and student activities:

  • Observation Activity (Class 8–9): Bring samples of garden soil. Students examine soil texture, smell, and moisture, then discuss how invisible fungi and microbes contribute to soil health.
  • Experiment (Class 11–12): Demonstrate the effect of Trichoderma using two sets of seeds—one treated with a biofungicide and one untreated. Compare germination and growth.
  • Discussion prompt: Why are biological solutions like fungi-based biofungicides better than chemical pesticides? Students can debate the advantages and limitations.
  • Case study analysis: Share a short news article on pesticide resistance. Students propose how beneficial fungi could solve the issue.
  • Creative task: Ask students to design an awareness poster or infographic titled “Silent Protectors of Plants”.

Teacher tip: Use this article as a bridge to discuss sustainability, linking science concepts with environmental responsibility.

Dr. Andleeb Zehra is a biology educator at Crimson Anisha Global School, Pune, with experience in CBSE and IGCSE boards. She has published articles on microbes, emphasizing their role in biology, agriculture, and health. With expertise in microbiology, she fosters curiosity, research-based learning, and deeper understanding of the natural world.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Dr. Andleeb Zehra
When we hear the word ‘fungi’, the immediate feeling that probably pops up in your mind is that of disgust. But fungi, quite a few of them, can be beneficial to plants and help them remain healthy. Learn about biofungicides and their uses in this article.

item.title: Transforming education: a new era for schools
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Ujala Sawant

If I could change something in education, it would be the following:

  • Uniform timings: Early morning timings with reduced school hours will ensure that students get enough time for other activities.
  • Celebrating birthdays in traditional ways: Celebrating children’s birthdays according to our traditions, whether it is bringing kheer to class or taking the blessings of elders is a good way to introduce children to our customs and values.
  • Project-based learning: Textbooks should include projects based on visits to local historical sites along with parents.
  • Regular PE and library classes: The timetable should include regular physical education and library classes to help develop physical activity and reading skills in students.
  • Lost recipes: Students should bring lost recipes in their lunch boxes to both bring back traditional foods and healthy eating habits.
  • Wall of humanity: Schools should have a "Wall of Humanity", where students can donate books, toys, clothes, and dry ration.
  • Civic sense awareness: Each school must conduct civic sense drives. A generation of students with strong civic sense can lead to a cultural shift, where community service, environmental conservation, and social responsibility become valued norm.
  • Focus on practical education: Schools should switch to practical learning, because we are moving into a digital India, where application based learning is the need of the hour.

Impact of these changes

  • Improved academic performance: By focusing on practical skills and critical thinking, students will become more engaged and motivated.
  • Enhanced overall development: Increased parental involvement, cultural awareness, and environmental consciousness will contribute to well-rounded individuals.

To bring these reforms to the larger education system, the government should:

  • Implement a uniform fee structure: Ensure accessibility and inclusivity by minimizing education fees.
  • Recognize best practices: Award schools that adopt innovative and effective practices.
  • Empower teachers: Provide special facilities and recognition to teachers.
  • Promote cultural exchange programmes: Cross-cultural understanding and collaboration between enducational institutions can be enhanced through government-sponsored programmes.

Conclusion

By implementing these reforms, we can create a more inclusive, practical, and culturally aware education system. By working together, we can empower the next generation with the skills, knowledge, and values necessary to thrive in an ever-changing world. The government should play a proactive role in promoting these reforms and recognizing the efforts of schools and teachers.

Ujala Sawant is an educator at Bharati Vidyapeeth Engiish Medium High School, Dhankawadi Pune. She can be reached at ujalasawant2330@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Ujala Sawant
Being in the field, the best people who can tell us what changes should be made to education to make it a more successful system are teachers. Here is one teacher’s wish list.

item.title: Learning about learning: Insights from an educational workshop conducted with youth living with HIV
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Arun Shet, Bindu Thirumalai, and Michael Babu Raj

Learning how we learn is a game-changer. Metacognition—the process of understanding how we acquire, process, and retain information— isn’t just a fancy term, it’s the secret sauce to mastering any skill and improving executive function. It is also key to achieving self-regulation, whereby students manage study activities by themselves. Facilitating the acquisition of metacognitive skills, when executed artfully, can provide students with a clear path through the beautiful and somewhat chaotic mess of learning. When we understand how our brains absorb and apply information, we unlock the power to learn smarter, adapt faster, and grow endlessly. Young people living with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) face more adversity than their uninfected peers. Yet, they desire to learn effectively and are eager to give back to the world.

At RISHI Foundation, an educational nonprofit organization in South India that implements an experiential learning program for youth living with HIV, we conducted a dynamic social and emotional learning workshop. The event was not just a training session; it became a transformative experience where participants discovered the power of self-awareness, goal-setting, and critical thinking. The enthusiasm and curiosity of participants was palpable, as they actively engaged in discussions, activities, and reflective exercises that left a lasting impact on their minds. At the conclusion of the workshop, several participants reflected upon a growing desire to implement the new learning within their daily activities as peer mentors of other HIV affected individuals.

Learning to learn: a journey of discovery: Participants explored how the concept of metacognition—thinking about one's thinking-- could provide a scaffold for the learning process, by helping retain information more effectively and develop lifelong learning skills. Time management, specifically using techniques like the 1-3-5 method, brought clarity to their daily routines, while a SWOT analysis encouraged self-reflection to identify strengths and areas for growth. SMART goal setting surfaced as a favourite topic. Several youth emerged from this experience with clear, actionable goals and a sense of purpose. Understanding how to set realistic and measurable objectives instilled a newfound confidence in their abilities.

Personal growth in action: Suhas, a participant, shared how the "Rocks, Pebbles, and Sand" model reshaped his perspective on task management. He learned to distinguish between essential priorities and minor distractions, ultimately reducing stress and improving focus. Meena, who once feared failure, discovered how a growth mindset could be empowering. Through engaging discussions and hands-on activities, she now views challenges as opportunities rather than obstacles. "Mistakes don't define me," she says. "They teach me."

The power of reflection: Journaling emerged as a simple yet profound practice for the participants. Gowthami found clarity and stress relief through daily writing, turning her reflections into actionable insights for personal growth. The workshop also encouraged discussions on career planning, sparking interest in livelihoods for long-term success. Meghana appreciated learning about resume building, while others were inspired to seek mentorship and networking opportunities.

Insights for lifelong learning: Feedback from the learning workshop was overwhelmingly positive, with participants asking for more interactive activities, videos, and outdoor sessions. They also expressed interest in exploring topics like mental health, career development, and gender empowerment. This workshop wasn't just about strategies and tools—it was about igniting a passion for learning and boosting student engagement. Previously disengaged participants identified in themselves a desire to implement the new learning within their daily activities as peer mentors of other HIV affected individuals. Two weeks later, three workshop participants led a women’s health concerns training session for HIV affected girls, during which they implemented their new-found knowledge.

As one participant put it, "The sessions were so meaningful and practical. I feel more confident to face challenges and take control of my future." Participants demonstrated self-efficacy that was in some ways underappreciated even by the organizers of this workshop.

Childcare organizations working with underprivileged children and youth that primarily engage in self-directed learning, should consider fostering their learning with metacognitive and procedural scaffolding techniques. This may ensure that the citizens of tomorrow develop social and emotional learning skills and acquire the confidence to become effective learners that shape their futures with courage and hope.

Take-home messages

  • Prioritize wisely: Focus on your "rocks"—the most important tasks.
  • Embrace mistakes: View failures as growth opportunities.
  • Practice self-reflection: Journal to track progress and gain insight.
  • Be proactive: Own your career development.

Arun Shet is a physician and cofounder of RISHI Foundation, a nonprofit organization involved in education, mentoring, and skilling of orphans and vulnerable children in Bengaluru.

Bindu Thirumalai has a master’s degree in education and a doctoral degree from Tata Institute of Social Sciences. Her thesis involved an action research study on Teachers’ online communities of practice. Her areas of interest are Teacher Education, EdTech, Math Pedagogy, and Design Thinking in Education.

Michael Babu Raj has a doctoral degree in human rights and has completed a Teach for India fellowship. He is the program director at RISHI Foundation, and leads the I’mPossible Fellowship two year training program for youth living with HIV.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Arun Shet, Bindu Thirumalai, and Michael Babu Raj
Knowing how we learn goes a long way in helping us learn better giving us the ability to chart our futures with confidence. The RISHI Foundation used metacognitive and procedural scaffolding techniques during a workshop with some youth affect by HIV. What they learnt at this workshop is something that will benefit all human beings.

item.title: In gratitude: what my teachers taught me about teaching and life
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Aniruddha Jena

As the first week of September arrives, I often pause to reflect on the teachers who have shaped my life, not just those who stood at the front of a classroom but also those whose mentorship, kindness, and insistence on excellence have left a lasting mark on my journey.

Teacher’s Day has always been a curious occasion. As a student, it was a day of flowers, handwritten cards, and hurriedly prepared cultural programmes; as a teacher now, it has a different meaning. It is no longer only about celebration. It is a moment of reckoning, a time to hold up a mirror to my own practice and ask: Am I giving my students what my teachers gave me?

Some of the most enduring lessons I have learned were not part of any syllabus. I remember my late mother often said to me in Odia, “Ek Lekha Sahe Baka,” which translates to: you might read and recite something a hundred times, but if you can write it clearly even once, it carries far more substance and impact. She believed that once written, words are etched for time. This insight has stayed with me, shaping my academic writing and my approach to communication, more broadly. When I was a master’s student at the Central University of Odisha, Koraput, one of my professors encouraged me to start writing short ‘letters to the editor’ in newspapers. I remember failing to get a single letter published for more than three months despite daily submissions. Then one day, two letters appeared in print, and that moment sparked my enduring love for writing. My professor suggested this because he knew I came from a vernacular medium background and wanted to help me bridge the gap. Seeing my byline in print was transformative and became a vital step in my journey.

Later, during my PhD days, I encountered professors who showed that scholarship is about sharing knowledge generously. One of my professors at the University of Hyderabad more recently, during a particularly difficult period when I was cautioned for writing something perceived as anti-establishment, told me,“Aniruddha, you, as a young academic with much to give to students and the academic world, should look at things differently. You shouldn’t feel compelled to wear your values and opinions on your sleeves to be acceptable in ‘progressive’ circles. No need to be a flag-waving, chest-thumping dissident academic. That doesn’t mean you compromise your values. Ultimately, when you get up in the morning and look at yourself in the mirror, you shouldn’t feel embarrassed by what you are looking at, professionally or personally.” Another professor, when I accepted my first teaching job in 2021, said,“As teachers we always look for that ‘wow’ moment in class and after class. We prepare and do our homework. But it doesn’t always happen that way. Some days, even with the greatest preparation, you might not experience it, and some days it comes unexpectedly. What matters is how well you prepared and tried to convey your ideas meaningfully, and whether you helped students and facilitated them enough to meet the objectives of the class collectively, while creating room for both individual and collective growth.” These words, along with many other lessons, taught me that intellectual humility is as vital as intellectual rigour. In academia, where publishing, competition, and metrics can overshadow the joy of discovery, this lesson has been grounding.

Even outside formal education, I have been mentored by colleagues and senior professionals whose quiet support during moments of self-doubt made a difference. These teachers, in the broadest sense, reminded me that the human connection in teaching is as important as the content itself.

When I became a teacher, I realized much of what I valued in my mentors cannot be taught in the conventional sense. It is not enough to master the subject; one must also cultivate patience, openness, and the ability to listen deeply. I have learned that teaching is, at its best, a reciprocal process. The classroom is not a one-way transfer of knowledge from the “knowing” to the “unknowing.” It is a dynamic space where the teacher is also a learner, shaped by the questions, perspectives, and even the silences of students.

Over time, I have also come to appreciate the moral dimension of teaching. We live in an era of rapid change, where students navigate shifting social values, economic uncertainties, and the constant noise of media. The role of a teacher is not to provide easy answers, but to equip students with the tools to think critically, engage ethically, and respond compassionately. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted this in unexpected ways. Like many educators, I had to shift to online teaching almost overnight. The challenges were many: the absence of non-verbal cues, the inequities of digital access, and the emotional toll of uncertainty. Yet, in those disembodied Zoom/Google Meet grids, I saw resilience, adaptability, and creativity in my students and myself. It reaffirmed my belief that the heart of teaching is relational, not transactional. Whether in a physical classroom or a virtual one, it is the sense of being seen, heard, and valued that makes learning possible.

In recent years, the role of the teacher has become both more visible and more undervalued. Visible because education is increasingly seen as a driver of societal progress. Undervalued because the emotional labour, preparation, and pastoral care that teaching demands often go unrecognized.

Yet, the need for teachers who can navigate this complex terrain has only grown. In a world overcrowded with information but often starved of wisdom, the teacher remains a crucial figure, not merely as a transmitter of knowledge, but as a guide, a challenger, and sometimes a much-needed anchor.

In academic life, we often talk about “standing on the shoulders of giants.” For me, these giants are not only eminent and accomplished scholars; they are also the schoolteacher and college lecturer who noticed my awkward shyness and encouraged me to speak, the professor who returned my drafts with changes all tracked but with a smile that said, “You can do better,” and the colleague who quietly reassured me that a failed job application was not a failed career.

On this Teacher’s Day, I will not be waiting for flowers or greetings from my students. Instead, I will be sending silent thanks to those who shaped my thinking, honed my craft, and taught me by example that the measure of a teacher’s success is not in the accolades they receive, but in the lives they touch.

And perhaps, in the quiet spaces between lectures and deadlines, I will ask myself again: Am I living up to the legacy they left me?

Aniruddha Jena is Assistant Professor in the Communications Area at the Indian Institute of Management Kashipur, Uttarakhand. His work explores the intersections of media, communication, and society. He can be reached at stapanjena@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Aniruddha Jena
Every September 5th, students celebrate teachers, make them feel special, shower them with gifts and cards and treat them to entertaining programmes. But what does Teacher’s Day mean to teachers themselves? A teacher writes.

item.title: They remember you, not the syllabus
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Mahima Samanth

When I first stepped into a classroom as a chemistry teacher, I carried a head full of equations, theories, and lesson plans. I imagined students remembering my meticulous explanations and blackboard diagrams. But later, when I met my former students, what they recalled wasn't chemistry — it was connection. They remembered not my lectures, but my presence.

Belief is the first lesson

One student once told me, "Ma'am, I still remember how you smiled and encouraged me when I was nervous during my seminar — you made me believe I could speak." It wasn't polymers or corrosion that stayed with her. It was a moment of quiet confidence I helped build. That’s when I realized students carry our belief in them longer than they carry our notes.

Emotional chemistry over chemical reactions

A student who avoided asking questions once heard me say, “Your doubts are valid. Don’t compare your pace with others.” He didn’t respond immediately, but later wrote to me, “That one sentence changed how I saw myself.” It reminded me that while we teach chemical reactions, it's our human reactions that last the longest.

Support beyond syllabus

A student who often struggled with attendance once said, "Ma’am, thank you for not giving up on me." It wasn’t about marks. It was about being seen without judgment. Another student once remarked – “No one compares to you -- you are different”. You felt like a family member he said. You allowed us to speak freely and quietly saved us from many situations he added. The words had nothing to do with experiments or exams. They were all about emotional safety.

Moments that say "you matter"

Once during a lecture, I paused after noticing a student in visible distress. We spoke after the class. Later, she recalled that moment: "You reminded me I mattered beyond marks and mistakes." In another instance, a student confessed, "We always pray you’d be the exam invigilator — not to cheat, but because your presence calmed us."

Gifts of gratitude

One day, a former student returned with a saree as a birthday gift. There were no grades left to earn. She just said, "Thank you for being there." That saree was more than just fabric-- it was gratitude woven in thread.

Becoming their safe space

There were times when students came not with doubts but with fears — family troubles, personal losses, or deep insecurities. One said, “Ma’am, I just wanted you to know first.” In that moment, I wasn’t a chemistry teacher. I was a safe space. That is what students need the most — someone who listens and who sees them beyond their academic identity.

When presence matters most

I once noticed a student walking silently through the corridor, lost in thought. I gently asked, “Are you okay?” He later said, “Even my friends didn’t notice,but you did.” That moment stayed with both of us. Because sometimes, noticing is the most powerful teaching.

Do teacher-student bonds expire?

After a semester ended, some students visited, clearly emotional. "Ma’am, we feel jealous seeing you teach others," one said. Another added, "In chemistry, old bonds break when new ones form.” Did our bond break too? Even now, they message and ask if I can come back. Their words — "You were ours first!" — echo with affection. When students fight for your presence, it shows that the bond isn’t bound by syllabus or schedule.

The real legacy

Even today, I get calls or messages — not for help with chemistry, but just to share a heartbreak or success. They remember my presence, not my presentations. And maybe that’s the ultimate reward: knowing that even if they forget the syllabus, they remember the care.

Mahima Samanth is a chemistry professor at an engineering college with a deep interest in value-based education and student wellbeing. Along with her teaching responsibilities, she also writes and researches. She has published several review articles and currently serves as a reviewer for reputed journals under Springer and the American Chemical Society. Her writings reflect a profound understanding of the subtle emotional and ethical dimensions of education, coupled with a sensitive outlook and experiential perspective. She firmly believes that education is not merely the transmission of knowledge, but a process of imparting values, inspiration, and a vision for life.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Mahima Samanth
This Teacher’s Day, a teacher reflects on what it means to truly be a teacher.

item.title: How Notebook LM transformed my classroom engagement
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Sravanthi Datla

As an educator, one of my primary goals is to create an engaging, dynamic, and effective learning environment for my students. However, with so much content to teach and learn and with varying learning styles in my classroom, maintaining student interest while ensuring understanding can be a challenge. That’s when I discovered Notebook LM, a cutting-edge tool powered by AI, which has completely changed the way I engage with my students. Notebook LM is an experimental AI-first notebook developed by Google. It uses the documents you upload to train a specialized AI, which then becomes an expert on your documents, giving you an invaluable resource, specific to your needs. This tool has allowed me to provide personalized, interactive, and multifaceted learning experiences that enhance class discussions, facilitate better understanding, and foster deeper engagement.

Let me walk you through how Notebook LM has revolutionized my classroom, particularly in areas such as study guides, interactive podcasts, resource-based recommendations, FAQs, and historical timelines.

Interactive study guides that reinforce learning

One of the most powerful features of Notebook LM is its ability to create highly tailored study guides for my students. In the past, I used to distribute static study guides that might not have addressed individual learning gaps. Now, with Notebook LM, students are provided with personalized study guides that adapt based on their progress and needs.

For example, if a student is struggling with a particular concept, the AI-generated study guide will suggest extra resources, offer simpler explanations, and even quiz the student on difficult concepts. On the other hand, if a student already has a solid grasp of the material, the guide will challenge them with more advanced questions or direct them to further reading. This level of personalization has been incredibly helpful in making sure that every student has the resources they need to succeed.

Moreover, because the study guides are interactive, they allow students to engage with the material in a more hands-on manner. Students can actively test their knowledge, receive immediate feedback, and track their progress. This interactive approach has significantly boosted student confidence and preparedness for exams, as they no longer just memorize facts, but understand the underlying concepts.

Engaging interactive podcasts for active learning


Another feature I’ve come to love is the interactive podcast function of Notebook LM. Podcasts are a great tool for reinforcing learning outside of the classroom, and with Notebook LM, students can listen to podcasts that are tailored to the specific content they’re studying. These podcasts aren't just passive listening experiences—they incorporate interactive elements that encourage students to engage with the material as they listen.

For example, while listening to a podcast on a historical event, students are prompted to pause the podcast and answer reflection questions, solve related problems, or contribute to a live discussion thread. This kind of engagement makes the content more memorable and helps students process the information more deeply.

The beauty of this feature lies in its flexibility: students can access the podcasts on their own schedule, making learning more flexible while still promoting active participation. It also caters to auditory learners, allowing them to absorb information in a way that suits their learning style.

Providing the best resources based on student needs

Notebook LM doesn’t just generate study guides and podcasts; it also identifies the best resources based on the individual needs of students. For example, if a student is struggling to understand a particular concept in science, Notebook LM will search through textbooks, articles, videos, and other resources to recommend the most relevant and high-quality materials.

The AI continuously adapts and refines its recommendations based on the student's learning progress, ensuring that students are always exposed to the best content available. This approach ensures that resources aren't one-size-fits-all but are instead customized to meet the unique needs and learning pace of each student.

Having access to a curated selection of the best resources helps students stay focused and saves them time, so they can spend more time learning and less time sifting through irrelevant content. This personalized approach not only enhances student engagement but also deepens their understanding of the subject matter.

Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) based on resources

One of the most time-consuming tasks for any educator is answering the same questions repeatedly from students who may have missed a detail in class or who are struggling to grasp certain ideas. With Notebook LM, this problem is efficiently solved through the FAQ feature.

Notebook LM generates FAQs based on the content provided in the resources, so when students are reviewing the material on their own, they can quickly access a list of the most commonly asked questions and their answers. This proactive approach to answering questions ensures that students can find clarification on their own without having to wait for class discussions or one- on-one time.

The FAQ feature also works as a great revision tool. Students can use it to refresh their memory on key concepts, and since the AI continuously adapts, the FAQs evolve based on the ongoing content students are studying. This means that students receive answers that are most relevant to the material they are currently working on, allowing them to stay on track with their learning.

Creating historical timelines for better understanding of events

When teaching history, teachers often struggle with helping students visualize the sequence of events and understand the historical context. However, Notebook LM makes this process significantly easier by automatically generating historical timelines related to the topics being taught.

For example, if a teacher is teaching about the French Revolution, Notebook LM will create a detailed timeline of the key events -- Pre-1789: Seeds of the Revolution, 1789: The Revolution Begins, 1790-     1792: Towards a Republic-- while providing additional context and explanations. Students can see how different historical events are interconnected.

Helping students in every way possible

Ultimately, the biggest benefit of Notebook LM is how it supports students in every facet of their learning journey. Whether they are using personalized study guides to review for an exam, listening to interactive podcasts to reinforce class lessons, or using timelines to understand the chronology of historical events, Notebook LM makes learning engaging, accessible, and tailored to their needs.

The tool doesn’t just enhance student engagement; it also fosters deeper learning by providing multiple pathways for students to interact with the content, ask questions, and reflect on their progress.

Conclusion

Notebook LM has been a game-changer in my teaching practice, offering a wealth of resources that support a variety of learning styles. By integrating study guides, interactive podcasts, FAQs, and timelines into my classroom, I’ve seen a significant improvement in student engagement, comprehension, and retention. The AI-powered tool allows us to focus on what matters

most facilitating meaningful learning experiences—while providing students with the resources and support they need to thrive.

If you're looking to enhance classroom engagement and help your students in all the ways they need, I highly recommend giving Notebook LM a try. It’s truly the future of education in the digital age.    

Sravanthi Datla teaches science and is the Primary Coordinator at Westberry School, Bhimavaram, Andhra Pradesh. She can be reached at sravanthi.datla@westberry.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sravanthi Datla
Notebook LM is the latest tool that edtech brings teachers. Notebook LM is designed to assist teachers and help them make their classrooms a lot more engaging, purposeful, and suitable to different student learning styles. A teacher shares her experience.

item.title: Enlightenment comes from the curiosity to find answers
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Manjula Rao

Sometimes children see what adults do not and speak about it with confidence. What they say can be taken as intelligence or nonsense, depending on what the listener or reader understands of it.

All children are not alike. If a teacher is annoyed because a student has not understood the topic being taught that day, it does not mean that that student is disinterested or lazy. He may have some learning problems, which the teacher should find out patiently. Irritation can set fear in the child both towards the teacher and for the subject.

Instead, if the child has any strengths, he/she should be encouraged to take that up seriously and better himself/herself in that field. That will give the child confidence.

In this day and time, it should not be difficult to find solutions.

This is how curiosity gives enlightenment both to the teacher and the taught.

**********************************************************************************************************

Recently, I read a thank you note written to a chairperson visiting a school. The note said    

“Your presence brought a unique energy to the campus --one that inspired, uplifted, and deeply resonated with everyone you interacted with.

For our teaching and non-teaching staff, your visit was nothing short of a master class. The clarity of your thoughts and the depth of your approachable nature, created an atmosphere of meaningful learning and reflection.

What stood out most was your interaction with the parents. Your words were not only insightful but also soul-stirring. You encouraged them to embrace their children’s individuality, and support them in discovering their own paths. That moment left a mark on many hearts.”

“Leaving a mark” should be the purpose of people visiting or teaching in places where there are children. Every small gesture can leave a life-long impact on their minds. Just status and position to glorify oneself is not what children look for. Children always want someone who will appreciate their originality and creativity.    

**********************************************************************************************************

The age of technology has both advantages as well as disadvantanges. Technology can become addictive. Whether parents or teachers, we simply brush aside this addiction with, “Everyone is glued to technology nowadays.” But does that help? Parents blame the school, the school blames the teachers, the teachers blame the parents. Back to square one. The victims are the children. Is there any solution to this? Yes, there is.

Top schools have introduced the use of technology from kindergarten itself. The need to use such gadgets from such an early age is debatable. If classroom lessons are captivating and interesting and not entirely based on textbooks or a rigid, boring curriculum, the problems can be handled to a great extent.

I share below an example of how a teacher planned an integrated lesson. This is the popular Panchatantra tale about ‘The Lion and The Rabbit.’ The subjects covered are language, art, science, and performance skills.      

One group of children read the story out loud. Some students brought the story to life as they drew on the blackboard. A few students enacted the story as it was being read out. In this way, every child was involved and engaged.               

At the end of the story, these students, who were by now stimulated, asked-- What makes an echo? What is reflection in water? How does it differ from the reflection in a mirror? Why did the water splash? And many more questions.

Before getting into the details, the teacher asked some students to bring a mug, fill it with water and find a stone big enough to be dropped into the mug.

When the stone was dropped in the mug, from a height, some water splashed out of it. Why did the water splash out?

After a little time, one student, like the clever rabbit, raised a hand and excitedly said that the stone had taken the place of the water. It was displacement of water. Everyone clapped in appreciation.

They also appreciated the drawings on the board, some spoke of their acting, while others were complemented on their reading. And the discussion carried on. The teacher explained ‘echo’ and ‘reflection’.

Such lessons take away the boredom of a lecture or reading from books. Here, the students are making use of their various sense organs-- seeing, hearing, speaking, touching, and smelling.    

**********************************************************************************************************

Just as I was writing this article for Teacher Plus, I received a message with the following essay. The teacher marked the student 100 out of 100. How would you like to mark the student and why?

A teacher asked her 8th grade students to write an essay on “Thieves’. This is what one student wrote-

Thieves are also an important part of a nation’s economy. They play a significant role in providing employment and contributing to the nation’s development.

Safes, locks, lockers, cupboards, etc., are made only because of thieves. Many factories and workshops involved in making these items provide employment. Thanks to this profession.

Even in homes, masons and workers, get work installing latches, locks, grills on windows and doors.

Then to protect houses,      shops, schools, colleges, factories, guards are employed.

Companies that manufacture CCTV cameras, metal detectors and security systems generate jobs.

Because of thieves, police officers, court staff, judges, lawyers and others are employed.

Purchase of barricades, weapons, bullets,batons,uniforms, vehicles, and motor cycles for the police help boost the economy.

Thanks to thieves, jailors, jails, and prison staff have jobs.

When items like mobiles,laptops,cars, motorcycles, electrical appliances, purses, or lipsticks are stolen, people have to buy them again,which boosts business.

Much more could be said, but overall,the contribution of thieves,to a nation’s economy is noteworthy.”

Brilliant thinking, isn’t it?

If students are given an incentive for originality and thinking out of the box, the educational system may show unique results.    

Let parents, teachers, and children put one innovative thought up on the notice board, daily and see the result.

Take the children outdoors every day for 10 minutes of observation. Studying out doors is a great asset. There will be plenty of questions about clouds, rain, trees, flowers, water, mountains, birds, insects, shadows, etc. Curiosity leads to questions and questions lead to learning.    

Manjula Rao is a former teacher who enjoyed every moment of her career. She has taught in different schools, in different places, both girls and boys at different levels. The varied exposure from rural schools, NGO, missionary school, public schools, IB schools -- from the primary to the graduate level, including students with learning disabilities-- has helped her gain a lot of knowledge, which she shares whenever she can.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Manjula Rao
Four instances and four learnings from them. Manjula Rao writes about what true education should actually look like

item.title: How my childhood teacher shaped the way I teach today
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Fathima Binsiya M

Another Teacher’s Day is coming. In the hustle and bustle, we may forget to reflect on our memories and pen them down. Let us remember the people who moulded us.

Every time I walk through a noisy school corridor as a teacher, I’m transported back to my childhood classrooms—the laughter, the chaos, my friends, the teachers, and so many more things.

In those moments, I walk with a wistful smile from the heart. Among all those memories, the most precious and heartwarming one is of Ramla Teacher. Teachers truly have a profound influence on students' lives.

Now, as a teacher myself, I often draw inspiration from her approach—especially in how she built emotional connections with students and handled classroom challenges with grace.

Remembering my teacher

Ramla Teacher taught me the Urdu language from grade 5 to grade7. She was as sweet as

the ghazals she introduced us to. For me, she was more than just a teacher—I would say I learned Urdu because of my affection for her. Isn’t it often like that? When we like a teacher, we tend to learn the subject better. The Urdu letters and poems I still remember are a testament to her love and care.

Beyond lessons, I learned a great deal from her—especially about social and emotional learning, though it wasn’t part of our syllabus then. She could truly hear us by stepping into our shoes. A gentle pat on the back or her hand on our shoulders built a deep emotional connection. She acknowledged our successes, guided us softly when we were wrong, and never once raised her voice. Her tone was always calm and often carried through songs.

How I apply these lessons today

After becoming a teacher, I realized just how deeply she influenced me.

Firstly, listening to students. I believe we must be affectionate towards our students, especially in the elementary grades. Often, they have more to say than we do. Many disruptive students simply have a lot to express. When they misbehave, I try kind words first. If I do end up scolding them, I feel sad afterwards—so I make sure to follow up with a conversation. Simple

questions like “How are you?” or “What are your hobbies?” often reveal the real reasons behind their behaviour. These moments take me deeper into their lives and help me support them as best I can.

Secondly, to be both a teacher and a friend. A little appreciation makes children immensely happy. When I give a warm pat on the back or let them sit with me for a while, I see sparks of joy in their eyes.

I admired how Ramla Teacher remained composed in difficult situations. This is something I’m still learning. At times, I raise my voice and then regret it. I’ve realized that emotional regulation is as important for teachers as it is for children. When I fall short, I reflect, learn, and try again.

My teacher was a wonderful singer. We eagerly waited for her songs, often more than the textbook poems themselves. I’m not a good singer, but I hum at the beginning of class or when it becomes too chaotic. It works far better than saying, “Please, be quiet” a hundred times. The room settles, and once I have their attention, I pause and begin the lesson. Humming has become one of my best classroom tools. We all have different talents—drawing, acting, dancing. Using them can be a powerful way to connect.

To educators

In this journey of education, we’ve all met teachers who do their job beautifully. Sometimes, it’s one person; other times, it’s many, each with different strengths. All we need to do is reflect on our own actions and keep learning.

We are moulding the future. Let’s look back and learn from the teachers who shaped us. There are always lessons—about what a good teacher should be, and sometimes, what a teacher should not be.

Even after 12 years, Ramla Teacher is still guiding me. Our classrooms may look different today, but the heart of teaching remains the same: connection, care, and calm.

Fathima Binsiya M is a Global Perspectives teacher at Aimee International School in Vijayawada, India. She is passionate about student-centered and experiential learning, and believes in nurturing both academic excellence and a strong sense of social commitment through meaningful classroom connections. She can be reached at mfbinsiya1@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Fathima Binsiya M
Fathima, now a teacher, reminisces about her school days and one teacher in particular. How do you remember your teacher?

item.title: A quest
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Jayanthi Sridhar

The question, “If you could change education, what would it be? And why?” to a teacher becomes a quest. A quest to reflect on what’s working well and what needs to change. A slew of issues – AI, dysfunctional families, climate crisis, unsafe environments, overdose of social media, easy access to the virtual world and money, and substance abuse – overwhelm children, throwing their lives out of gear. Addressing this at all levels is important for society to thrive and show collective progress.

An institution is more than a place of learning; it’s a place where values are built, strengthened, and sustained for life.

This article is an attempt to capture solutions using time-tested techniques and borrowed inspiration. Strategies are aplenty; it is important to look at what is feasible within the framework of an institution.

Clarity: Vision

It’s imperative that schools have a clear vision and have the entire fraternity aligned to this vision. There are many ways this can be done. Spending time on each value with appropriate anecdotes taken from a school’s growth chapter can be given as a talk or an activity-based workshop. Time should be given to absorb and understand the overall vision, and then the on-boarding process of teachers and parents can happen.

Alignment: Values

As individuals we all have a value system. It is necessary to know if our values align with the values of the school we work in, or where our children study. Schools can have a comprehensive session on nurturing values and relationships and on effective parenting styles a few times during the academic year.

The space: Layout

A school ideally must have trees, spaces that encourage gardening, soil, rooms suffused with natural light, and space for contemplation.

Ambience: Classroom

If there is flexibility to arrange furniture in the classroom according to the topic being studied, it might enhance learning. Groups of students can take up the responsibility of maintaining cleanliness and assisting the helpers and gardeners. Age-appropriate thought boards, reflective questions, and quiz questions with answers revealed after a few days can create a culture of critical thinking.

Classrooms can have desks with surfaces that can be written on with markers. Encourage students to use sketch notes to explain concepts. Classes can be taken outdoors twice during the day.

Learning: Curriculum

Teachers can be trained to be content creators and curriculum designers. Curriculum can be flexible depending on the class, and content can be made simpler or more challenging. Children should feel happy about their classes. There is often a mismatch between what we think is relevant and what actually works for children in their later years. We cannot predict the future; we can only prepare students to face challenges and think rationally with the excellent intuitive qualities they are already endowed with.

The curriculum can include activities that allow children to tap into their body’s wisdom and the universal intelligence. For this, teachers have to undergo training in spirituality and not just academics.

Here’s a beautiful instance of how a mentor nudges his students to observe while out in Nature:

“Do you feel that?” he asked, bouncing a little on his toes. “That sponginess underfoot? The humus here is extraordinary – millennia of decay. Imagine the rain of organic matter – needles, spores, twigs, and bugs building the soil from the top down. Photosynthesis – now isn’t that a pure wonder, how light and air can end up as soil?”He liked to remind us that the genus Homo is derived from the Latin word for “humus” and for “humility.” He was a humble man, a man of the soil who signed his name not with PhD appended, but as Francis D. Hole, TNS. It always drew the question, “Why TNS?”

TNS is an acronym for Temporarily Not Soil.

The above paragraph is an excerpt from an essay that is part of a series shared in partnership with the Center for Humans and Nature, from their five-volume collection, Elementals.

Children and adults are disconnected from Nature and natural rhythms. This can be re-established by allowing them to play, observe, listen, and reflect upon this vital connection we all share.

When children are allowed to touch soil and grow a garden, there is reverence for that precious layer of earth – soil. When they use their hands to make or build something, their confidence levels rise.

Hands-on activities like craftwork, gardening, needlework, different painting styles, and pottery can be included in the curriculum to power their potential.

Judicious usage: Technology

Children are highly vulnerable and are exposed to unseen dangers more than ever.

Lateral and vertical reading can be taught to students so that they gain insights from multiple resources and use their discretion to analyze and reason about the information they receive. Spotting red flags in what they consume as content every day, preparing students to take control and use their sense of judgment rather than rely on influencers or AI algorithms to manage their opinions, and shaping their sense of style and wellbeing are what teachers can work on.

Allowing children to connect with Nature, revere all creations, and giving them adequate tools for mental health like breath-work, mindfulness, gratitude journaling, yoga, and free play is vital. Grounding activities that are age-appropriate can be included, irrespective of the space available.

Schools that implement woodwork, weaving, and pottery by inviting experts and artisans show remarkable progress in thinking skills among students – their interactions with people improve as they understand that expertise takes years of dedication and perseverance.

Reading, writing, and recall can be revived in different formats. Reading spaces and book launches by students will encourage children to write and understand the efforts in organizing events and the need to slow down and experience time unplugged.

Peer-to-peer learning, and seniors and juniors learning from one another, can be included in the curriculum. Assessment for and by students using online tools will keep the students involved and engaged in the learning.

Leverage: Music

People tune into healing frequency music as a natural pathway to inner harmony. This ancient practice offers a fascinating intersection of science, sound, and therapeutic healing.

The chirping of birds, the rustling of leaves in the wind, or the falling rain are considered healing sounds. The frequencies of sound waves, measured in hertz (Hz), have healing properties.

The frequency of 528 Hz is believed to promote healing and repair at the cellular level, while a frequency of 432 Hz promotes balance and harmony. When healing frequencies are combined with music, the effects can be even more powerful.

Music classes or using music in classrooms and playing calming music in school buses might reduce anxiety levels and keep children happy.

Ancient texts like the Gandharva Veda (from the Vedic scriptures) and Raga Chikitsa elaborate on the healing properties of Indian music and the ability to raise consciousness and connect with the higher self.

Culture: Mantras and affirmations

“Live your life by a compass, not a clock.” – Stephen Covey.

Mantras strengthen focus and keep us grounded. Starting the day with a mantra, having students chant Annapurne Sadaapurne before food, and chanting the Shanthi Mantra at the end of the day are simple, doable practices that we follow in our school.

Simple affirmations such as, “I am committed to being mindful through my day,” which students resonate with, can be introduced before they begin the day along with shlokas to reinforce good, positive thinking.

Showcase: Special assembly

Every performance on stage gives children the opportunity to showcase any creative pursuit. Special assembly programs can be entirely scripted by students as young as class 4! This allows them to be articulate and polish their talent and organizational skills.

Skills: Clubs

Diverse skills by professionals can be taught to students. When an array of choices like photography, nature journaling, poetry, and linguistics are given, students become aware and adept at more skills. Learning the use of herbs as natural remedies, reviving forgotten traditional recipes, and taking part in solving civic issues are a few activities that can be offered to children throughout the year.

Mindfulness: Celebrations

School events that celebrate student wins should include connecting with the earth. The materials we use for decorations, the posters, the gifts, and the cutlery can be earth-friendly and become the voice for promoting a sustainable lifestyle.

We need to help children navigate the world and the issues they face by helping ourselves learn, adapt, and unlearn. Children should have safe, non-judgmental, extremely supportive spaces that enable a growth mindset and help them identify their purpose on this planet.

Children should be taught how to treat and interact with elders, not feel awkward around them. Our country’s ancient wisdom is as valuable as one can glean from it. To weave that wisdom in creative, scientific ways into the existing education system, currently a kaleidoscope of sorts, is the need of the hour. To empower children to reason, question, and, if necessary, disrupt obsolete norms and use their superpowers might sound fantastic. Yet, it is this optimistic view that drives teachers to come to school every day, sporting a smile, maneuvering through the emotions of young people, sensing the shift in how children learn, and still doing what’s best and permissible.

Sources

  1. https://www.weareteachers.com/how-to-talk-to-kids-about-war/
  2. https://www.nctsn.org/sites/default/files/resources/fact-sheet/talking-to-children-about-war.pdf
  3. https://www.greenchildmagazine.com/grounding-with-kids
  4. https://learningfundamentals.com.au/the-art-of-working-like-a-sprinter/
  5. DrMariaMontessori’s Handbook
  6. https://meditativemind.org/how-indian-classical-music-can-help-in-healing/
  7. https://www.myrelaxation.online/healing-frequencies

Jayanthi Sridhar is a Computer Science Facilitator at Sri Kumaran Children’s Home – CBSE, Bengaluru. She has a strong interest towards self-care practices and earth sensitive living. She conducts gratitude journaling sessions, likes to write on sustainability issues and education. She can be reached at jayanthi.sridhar@kumarans.org or you can visit her website https://journalmuse.net to know more about her work.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Jayanthi Sridhar
Can schools prepare children for AI, social media overload, and climate crisis – while also nurturing joy and resilience? Jayanthi Sridhar’s quest reimagines education.

item.title: Reading non-fiction and its benefits in the primary years
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Tanusree Dutt

I am an English teacher working with primary school children, and over the years I have seen how reading stories aloud to children and independent reading on their part shape the minds of children. Teaching at this stage of a child’s life is a privilege, because this is when curiosity is at its peak and habits of the heart and mind are formed.

I believe children should grow up reading all kinds of material—fiction, non-fiction, adventure stories, poems, factual texts, and even simple everyday writings—because every form of reading opens a new window of thought. Stories nurture imagination, poems awaken feelings, while informational texts build awareness of the world. Together, they help children become not only better readers, but also more thoughtful individuals. They learn to create their opinions about life and voice them out in the right forum.

In my classes I noticed that when children were given a variety of things to read, they became more engaged, asked more questions, and showed greater confidence in expressing themselves. It was no longer just about finishing a story or answering questions; reading became an adventure for them. That is why I feel strongly that children must be introduced to a wide range of reading materials right from the early years.

_______

Exploring non-fiction reading strategy

Over 5-6 weeks, I had the opportunity to implement targeted non-fiction reading strategies with a class of 27 students. This workshop was designed to foster critical thinking, deepen comprehension skills, and enhance the children’s ability to engage with informational texts effectively.

Making young readers deeply think about non-fiction books

The main purpose of reading non-fiction books is to gather information, evaluate ideas, ask questions, analyze, and reflect. As the learners of grade 2 started reading books of this genre, I noticed that they were reading them for fun and gathering little information. Through deliberate teaching and repeated practice, the children started to think differently about non-fiction books.

Starting to interpret and form an opinion

First, I taught the children how to identify the central claim in a non-fiction text. These were the guidelines I gave them:

  • Choose a non-fiction text.
  • What is the author trying to say?
  • Do I agree/disagree? Why or why not?
  • What do I already know about the topic?
  • How is this information changing or challenging my thinking?

To help readers understand what the author’s claim means, the following pointers were given:

  • It is their opinion.
  • The claim is what the author is trying to state and prove using facts and evidences.
  • The claim usually comes at the beginning of the text.

I demonstrated the process a few times before getting the children to do it themselves.

Why it is important to find the author’s claim while reading

It is important to understand what the author is trying to convey so that readers can evaluate and analyze that idea. It also helps them interact deeply and meaningfully with the text and ask questions, and form opinions. Moreover, it helps the learners use the claims as evidence while writing. Finally, it helps the readers differentiate between facts, opinions, and persuasion.

The children then tried it themselves and worked in groups to practice the same steps.

  • The children confidently voiced their opinions as they spoke to their partners, negotiated, challenged their thoughts, and formed an opinion. This included a lot of critical thinking and intense discussions.

Many students were able to say whether they agreed or disagreed with the author’s claims. While for one group that still struggled, the following questions were posed.

  • What was my initial belief?
  • What did I learn?
  • Did it change my thinking?

In this way, I was able to help the children understand that we don’t read only for fun. We read to collect ideas, change our perspectives, find evidence, and deeply think about what a book has to offer.

Wow moments leading to change

The most interesting moment in this journey was when I asked them, “What surprised you?” The children came up with interesting answers. One child said, “I didn't know whales could live so long.” Another said, “I was surprised to know that people used to believe that the Earth was flat.”

Over a period of time, my children started thinking deeply. They used their prior knowledge, started questioning the information they were receiving, and read other materials on the same topic to find out more for a better understanding, and finally reconsidered their previous assumptions. For example, one student said, “I used to think animals only adapt over hundreds of years, but now I see that some adaptations can happen much faster.”

After repeated practice, the children were slowly able to convert their perspectives based on the evidence found in the books they read.

How critical thinking while reading enhanced writing: the power of evidence-based persuasion

After several weeks of reading and practicing critical thinking with non-fiction texts, the children began incorporating evidence into their persuasive writing. They started using facts gathered from non-fiction books to support their opinions on books/movies/restaurants, and games, strengthening their arguments with evidence.

Example:

While writing a book review

From: This book teaches you a lot of things.

To: This book teaches you a lot of things. For example, in the story titled Oliver Button is a Sissy, Oliver never gave up. He practiced and practiced and performed on stage. It taught me never to give up.

This shift demonstrated a deeper understanding of how evidence strengthens an argument.

More samples:

Child 1 – First reason is, it has a great setting description. For example, for long on the nursery floor or the toy cupboard.

Child 2- Reasons 2-It has bravery. Splash isn’t afraid. She is ready for predators. Are you like that when camping?

Child 3-- First reason why you must read this book is that it is so funny. For example: Danny’s ladder and mine have nothing in between. The third reason is that there are a lot of new and smart words. You will learn words like bitter, security, thrilled, etc.

Strategies I will use going forward

Modelling

Using modelling and reflection, I can provide children with strategies to help them think while reading and allow them to build their comprehension.

Cross-text analysis:
It will help readers connect ideas across different sources and encourage critical thinking by comparing viewpoints.

Discussions led by learners:
I will encourage independent thinking and expression of ideas. This will also promote collaboration as children will share their interpretations.

Tanusree Dutt is an experienced educator with over 15 years of dedicated service in nurturing young minds and shaping foundational literacy skills in lower primary learners. As a teacher at Inventure Academy, she specializes in reading and writing skills, which she regards as essential gateways to lifelong learning, critical thinking, and self-expression. She can be reached at tanusree@inventureacademy.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Tanusree Dutt
When we thinking of children and reading, our minds usually stop at fiction. At the primary level, children are not too young to read non-fiction. Like with fiction, reading non-fiction can be very beneficial for children.

item.title: The classroom as story: what changes when we tell and listen
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Deeptha Vivekanand

“Your story of that little girl who faced her fears and finally overcame it changed my life and had a deep impact on me. I started loving storytelling and theatre because of you. Thanks again for making me a confident person that I never saw.”

This message, sent by a teacher after a storytelling session, made me sit up. Not because it praised my work – but because it reminded me what changes when a teacher begins to tell stories. This teacher lived every story she took to her classroom.

I’ve seen this shift countless times. A teacher stands up to tell a story, often with hesitation. “I’m not a performer,” she might say, or “I’m not good with voices.” But slowly, as she steps into the world of the story, something changes. She’s not just delivering content anymore. She is alive in the telling. And so are her students.

Storytelling does something no worksheet or whiteboard can. It humanizes. It draws people in. It invites vulnerability and expression. And for the teacher, it awakens a sense of voice – not just the literal voice, but the inner one that says, I have something worth sharing.

The storyteller inside the teacher

When a teacher begins to tell stories in the classroom, a transformation begins. This is not a performance skill. It is a pedagogical shift. I want to make a distinction here between telling and performing. Bringing a story to share with students to draw them into the lesson is not the same as acting it out. Storytelling in the classroom is about presence, intention, and connection – not theatrics. In telling stories, teachers who once focused on covering the syllabus begin to think in terms of narrative – sequence, suspense, metaphor, character. Their own understanding of the subject deepens. They move from explaining to evoking. From stating to connecting.

This change is often quiet but profound. I’ve seen teachers discover a joy they hadn’t felt in years. “I didn’t know I could hold a room like that,” one teacher said after her first storytelling session. Another told me, “My students actually listened without me asking them to.”

And that’s the magic of story. It invites attention, does not demand it. But it also invites something else that we rarely acknowledge in teacher training: play, emotion, and imagination. These are not distractions from learning. They are the foundation on which deep learning is built.

Listening is learning

But storytelling is only half the story. The other half – and just as important – is listening.

True listening is a learned skill. It requires focus, patience, and empathy. And in a world filled with distraction, teaching children to listen is one of the most radical acts a school can take on.

I’ve seen this impact echoed in the voices of students too. One student who was part of the school storytelling club told me: “It paved the way for me to explore not just different nuances of performing in front of an audience, but also made me a more informed consumer of the various kinds of literature. I was able to articulate my thoughts with more ease and got a better grip on the writings in my English textbook. It helped me dissect a piece of writing and internalize it better than I could before.”

Storytelling didn’t just build his confidence as an orator – it deepened his understanding, expression, and connection to literature.

In story-based classrooms, children don’t just sit and absorb. They visualize, predict, recall, retell, and reflect. They become participants in meaning-making. A child who listens well begins to connect dots, imagine alternatives, and relate emotionally to concepts, whether it’s a character in a folktale or a scientist making a discovery.

Which is why, I believe it’s time we go beyond using storytelling as a one-off tool, and embed it into the curriculum itself.

A story-based curriculum – moving from performance to process

We often say “learning should be joyful” but do we plan for joy? If we truly believe joy matters, it must reflect in how we design learning experiences. And storytelling gives us the tools to do that.

This is why I believe we must build a story-based curriculum, at least up to grade 8, where storytelling and listening are not side activities or co-curricular boxes to tick, but core pedagogical practices.

Too often, storytelling enters the school space only in the form of competitions. The goal becomes a medal or applause. While these events may build confidence, they also risk framing storytelling as a performance for others, rather than a process for oneself.

Storytelling is a way of knowing. A way of teaching. A way of learning. When a child retells a concept in their own words, they take ownership of it. When a teacher tells a story about a historical event, a scientific discovery, or even a math problem, they invite curiosity and connection.

A story-based curriculum might look like this:

  • History is taught through diaries, letters, and dramatized events that allow multiple viewpoints.
  • Science concepts are introduced through narrative dilemmas: Why did this happen? What did she discover? What was the turning point?
  • Maths becomes a series of puzzles, riddles, or real-life scenarios told through stories.
  • Legal studies explores laws through landmark cases told as courtroom stories, debates around rights and justice presented through dramatized hearings, or fictional narratives that raise ethical questions around legality.
  • Listening circles are part of every week – where teachers and children listen to and reflect on each other’s stories without judgment.
  • Assessment includes oral narration, illustrated retellings, or imaginative applications of a concept, alongside written tests.

This is not about replacing the syllabus or doing away with the textbook (although, that might be ideal!) – it’s about reframing it through story, so that both teacher and student engage with it more meaningfully.

What changes in the school?

When storytelling and listening are valued as core practices, the school culture itself changes. Classrooms become spaces of trust and imagination, not just instruction. Children who may struggle with textbook formats often thrive when they are invited to express what they’ve understood in story form. Teachers begin to collaborate across subjects, weaving shared narratives that cross disciplinary boundaries.

Most importantly, relationships deepen. Children start to listen to each other more. Teachers begin to notice nuances in how students respond. There is more joy. More connection. And a shared sense of we are learning together.

Is this scalable?

I believe it is because it doesn’t require expensive infrastructure or radical policy overhaul. It requires a shift in mindset. It asks us to value the imaginative and the emotional as much as we value the cognitive.

Many curricular frameworks already call for holistic, interdisciplinary, and creative approaches. Story is a natural vehicle for all of these. What we need now is teacher support and space – time to plan, co-create, and reflect on storytelling practices. We need training that focuses not just on voice modulation, but on how to find the story in every lesson.

The teacher as a listener too

The most beautiful stories I’ve heard from teachers are not just about what they told students – but about what they heard in return. A child retelling a folktale in their home language. A student drawing a comic strip of a science story. A quiet child who finally spoke up because the story made them feel seen.

And sometimes, a message like this: “Your story changed my life. I started loving storytelling and theatre because of you.”

When teachers begin to tell stories, they change. When children begin to listen and tell stories, learning becomes a shared act of imagination. When schools honour both, education becomes what it was always meant to be: a deeply human journey.

What if the most radical change we could make to education is also the oldest? What if we told more stories – and listened better?

Deeptha Vivekanand is the Storytelling and Reading Lead at a private school in Chandigarh. Her work centres on designing story-based learning experiences and supporting teacher development through reflective practice. She can be reached at deeptha.vivekanand@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Deeptha Vivekanand
What changes when teachers tell stories instead of just teaching their lessons? Deeptha Vivekanand presents beautiful possibilities.

item.title: Change this and you have changed it all
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

Why is teaching regarded as an “anybody can do it” profession? Worse, it is frequently the last choice for a job seeker: Didn’t get the job of your choice? No worries, you can always become a teacher.

It is THIS that I wish would change in our education system.
