Teaching is a demanding job and should be accorded due respect (not only on Teacher’s Day). Teaching is far more than transfer of prescribed content, or equipping students to succeed in the job market. As a teacher grows into her profession, she changes from within even as her students change her.

That the craft of teaching needs to be learnt, absorbed, immersed in, and allowed to slowly mature – how can our education system change so as to SEE this?

While I don’t have a simple formula to enable this transformation, I can describe indicators of a successful shift:

  • People who have never stepped into a classroom to teach will resist the temptation to advise a teacher how to teach. (Just as they will never tell a surgeon how to operate, or a lawyer how to win a case.)
  • Policy makers will include practising teachers in their meetings and factor ground realities into the policies they make.
  • Textbook writers will necessarily include practising teachers in their team of writers.
  • Management institutes and companies who have never entered school education will realize that they are not equipped to offer programs to school teachers and principals on how to do their jobs better.
  • Edtech companies will co-opt teachers into the development of their products instead of bypassing them totally as they now do.
  • Teacher preparation programs will not be the ultimate learning for a teacher. Instead, teachers will be seen as needing professional development all through their careers.
  • No teacher will be asked to go on census duty, election duty or gathering data for government surveys. [Instead, other professionals can be offered this privilege for a change.]
  • A teacher who finds her student needs to go for after-school tuition will sit up and realize that there is something deficient in her own teaching – and take immediate action to rectify this.
  • School managements will begin to encourage teachers to voice their opinions, speak their minds and think critically. [Unless they do this, they will not be able to expect teachers to hone critical thinking in their students.]
  • Teachers will be asked to choose their areas of development, instead of being forced to attend a mandatory set of annual workshops that may (not) align with their specific learning needs.
  • Teachers will have schedules that allow them to regularly spend time learning new things. Keeping teachers in the learning mode is the best way to ensure vibrancy in a school.

That’s my dream for a change in our education system. And I will settle even for four or five of these!

Neeraja Raghavan is Founder-Director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
Can education truly thrive if teachers aren’t seen as lifelong learners? Neeraja Raghavan imagines a system that could transform education.

item.title: Making space for compassion and clarity
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Swati Gautam

If I could change one thing in the education system, it would be to work towards nurturing empathetic individuals with a deep sense of gratitude. To achieve this, I would suggest that schools dedicate time for self-awareness and expression. I believe it is essential to ensure that every child is given the space to think and reflect. Looking at the world around us, it is clear that we need empathetic and sensitive individuals – those who can see beyond false media propaganda and view everyone as human beings, without any dividing lines.

To achieve this, we will need teachers who can think and reflect. Reflective writing practices or workshops should be made mandatory for teachers. When designing the B.Ed. curriculum or any teacher training course, it should include training that encourages critical thinking and reflection.

Circle time – (self-reflection and a safe space for expressing and sharing)

I feel that in the initial years of learning, children should be inculcated with the habit of gratitude, and that can only come through self-reflection and expression. Classrooms should include more circle time, and as teachers, we must create a safe space for listening. Listening with compassion and respect needs to be encouraged.

Schools should do away with memory-based, year-end summative assessments for younger children. Instead, assessments should be based on their everyday behaviour and interactions with those around them. They should be gently nudged to become more patient and empathetic.

I dream of classrooms where children are taught to reflect on their thoughts, on what they read, and on the world around them. I want children to be able to identify propaganda and not to blindly believe every piece of news they hear, but to fact-check, question, and reason. I want them to grow into thoughtful, independent individuals who can filter biases and see the world with clarity and compassion. Such individuals will undoubtedly develop a deep sense of empathy.

Space for discussion and dialogue

Children should have the space to express their views on various issues and events around the world. Teachers and students can together jot down ideas for mindful discussions where children are encouraged to raise their voices with respect and support their thoughts with facts. It is essential that they are heard patiently, and their views are received with dignity and openness.

When we speak of equality and social justice, we must begin early. Schools should be spaces that promote and nurture dialogue. Children must be given opportunities to meet, interact with, and understand those who are marginalized. It is crucial that they don’t grow up perceiving the poor or homeless as lazy or undeserving. These assumptions must be questioned, and children should be guided to explore the deeper causes of poverty and inequality.

I strive to bring relevance to every story, poem, or drama in my classroom. I encourage children to explore the underlying messages through discussion and inquiry, rather than spoon-feed them the answers. This approach allows their understanding to become personal and meaningful. Each child learns at their own pace and in their own way.

This practice fosters the ability to understand different points of view and helps children learn to respect differing opinions. Over time, it nurtures empathy and shapes them into compassionate human beings.

Practising gratitude

If every school in our country begins to nurture a mindset of gratitude, through small but meaningful classroom practices, our children will not only grow academically, but also into humane, aware, and grateful citizens.

This should become a conscious, national policy direction in education. Across all schools, rural or urban, government or private, we need to intentionally create space for practicing gratitude, empathy, and responsibility through everyday learning.

In my own school, especially in my Hindi classes, I have been trying to sow the seeds of this change. I encourage students to notice the people who support them every day – the support staff who cook, clean, maintain the gardens, and create a safe space. I want them to be self-aware and internally motivated, rather than simply respond to external pressures. My goal is to nurture thoughtful individuals who act with fairness and sensitivity.

While we focus on the basics – reading, writing, and comprehension – we also spend time asking “why”: Why are we learning this? Why is it important? How does it relate to the world around us? These questions ground learning in real-world relevance and spark empathy.

I think each school should try to bring the real world into the classroom and help children learn basic human characteristics like gratitude. If this becomes a national priority, our schools can become the beginning point for a more humane, just, and sensitive society.

Building empathy through storytelling

Stories are a powerful tool for communication. Children listen and see their own lives mirrored back. Without being told what to think, they draw their own meanings. This allows them to reflect, navigate their emotions, and build inner resilience. It also encourages critical thinking, often more effectively than direct instruction.

Personally, I avoid ending a story with a moral like, “So the lesson is…” because that narrows the child’s imagination. Let them absorb the story and interpret it in their own way. When we push a conclusion, we risk turning the story into a lecture instead of an experience.

Stories are the most powerful medium I know for teaching difficult or sensitive concepts. Whether the goal is to explain, reflect, or simply hold a safe space for emotions – stories work. They provoke thought, stir feelings, and open dialogue. Most importantly, stories build bridges between people, experiences, and hearts.

As teachers, we must allow stories to help children’s imaginations soar. In those skies of wonder, critical thinking and compassion take flight together. When a child listens deeply to a story – not just to listen, but to feel and understand – they become more human, more whole.

If I had the opportunity to make lasting changes to the education system, I would integrate all of the above as essential elements for cultivating gratitude and empathy in young minds. The world today urgently needs compassionate, sensitive individuals – and schools must not remain mere factories producing subject experts who lack emotional awareness or human connection.

Instead, schools should become spaces where essential human values are lived and practiced every day. If we truly want a world free of conflicts and boundaries, we must begin by nurturing emotional intelligence from a young age: encouraging critical thinking and questioning; enabling children to connect and empathize with people from all social backgrounds; grounding learning in real-world relevance; making language education a joyful, creative, and expressive experience; and fostering a deep concern for the environment.

Most importantly, we must remind ourselves that education is not just about what we know, but about who we become.

These changes, I believe, will raise a generation of conscious, kind, and courageous individuals – young people who won’t merely adapt to society, but who will shape it with compassion and clarity.

Swati loves teaching children. Each child she teaches holds a special place in her heart. A poet, storyteller, and proud mother of a confident 26-year-old lawyer daughter, she values learning, empathy, gratitude, and self-awareness as essential elements of her teaching journey. She can be reached at swatigautam@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Swati Gautam
What if schools measured empathy and gratitude as much as marks, asks Swati Gautam. She writes that education is not just about what we know, but about who we become.

item.title: Less is more: rethinking education for a changing world
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Vignesh Krishnan

What is strikingly common between children studying in vastly different corners of India? Let’s imagine the lives of two 15-year-olds – Saba, attending a private school in Bengaluru, Karnataka and Arjun, enrolled in a government school in Latehar, Jharkhand. Their classrooms, teachers, and daily realities may look nothing alike. But what they learn in school follows a similar pattern, a weight carried forward from past generations. The education system operates like a hoarder's house: nothing ever gets thrown away, everything just gets piled on top. If Priya or Arjun's grandparents learned about the Sepoy Mutiny of 1857 in the 1960s, their parents added computer awareness in the early 1990s. Now, they’ve inherited all of it plus environmental studies, plus value education, plus AI basics, among others.

This "do more, learn more" is so ingrained that not overloading kids just seems like a good wishlist that’s never seen the light of the day for decades. But is all this ‘more’ actually helping children as they grow into young adults? We see worrying signs everywhere: Millions of highly educated youth remain underemployed or jobless. In 2018, over 93,000 candidates, including 3,700 PhD holders, applied for 62 peon jobs in Uttar Pradesh that required only a 5th grade education (Economic Times, 2018). Similarly, former RBI Governor Raghuram Rajan highlighted that 19 million people applied for 60,000 railway jobs (The Print, 2019).

Meanwhile, we are also witnessing teenagers without degrees launching startups and leading climate movements. A student who started a side-hustle at 16 turned it into a $3 million business by her early 20s (Entrepreneur, 2020). Who doesn’t know about the impact 15-year-old Greta Thunberg had in kicking off a global youth climate activist movement. Gen Z and Gen Alpha are clearly eager to take on real-world challenges, but our education system seems stuck preparing them for a world that no longer exists.

The education-workforce disconnect

A glaring reality that is visible in the last few decades is that while there is a huge surge of academic degrees among young people, employers struggle to find job-ready talent. A recent national employability report revealed that only 42.6% of Indian graduates are actually employable, declining from 44.3% two years prior (Business Standard, 2024). More than half of college graduates do not meet the skill requirements of available jobs. The report pinpoints a widening gap between what universities produce and what industries need, especially as automation and AI reshape work.

Why are our graduates struggling? As Sameer Joshi, author and a consultant, observes: "We reward kids for regurgitating textbook answers, then wonder why they struggle with creative thinking later on." The academic path rewards absorbing vast content and scoring in standardized tests, rather than learning how to learn or applying knowledge creatively.

Curriculum overload: adding without subtracting

For decades, our education system simply piled on more material without cutting down the outdated parts. Educational institutions rarely remove topics for fear of leaving something out; instead, they stretch the plate wider.

In 2018, the then Union HRD Minister admitted that the school syllabus had become more exhaustive than some college courses, calling it "cramped" and in urgent need of reduction (Indian Express, 2018). "From the academic session 2019, the NCERT syllabus will be reduced to half," he announced. Yet meaningful reduction has been slow and sporadic.

The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 explicitly recognized the problem, recommending that curriculum content be reduced to core essentials to make space for critical thinking and inquiry-based learning (PIB, 2020). The policy says: Teach less, but teach it better. Focus on "key concepts, ideas, applications and problem-solving" rather than trying to cover everything.

However, turning that into reality is challenging. States often treat curricula as political battlegrounds. Consider the recent debate over the "three-language formula." The NEP proposed that children learn three languages to foster multilingual skills, but states like Tamil Nadu resisted, arguing that imposing a third language would burden students. Every additional compulsory subject crowds out time that could be spent mastering the basics or exploring personal interests.

Milind Nagda, educator and entrepreneur, captures this perfectly. He points out that while much needs to change in education, from how assessments are designed to how classrooms engage students, one of the most critical shifts is integrating design thinking and life-skills into the curriculum. For him, these are essential ingredients of an education system truly equipped for the 21st century. This overload has tangible consequences. Children today face cognitive loads that older generations can barely imagine. Their attention is pulled in so many directions that deep learning becomes difficult. Moreover, the relevance of the much mandated content is questionable. A few years from now, most facts they memorized will be irrelevant or easily looked up by AI, while skills they desperately need (critical thinking, managing people, etc.) might fall through the cracks.

The exam obsession: more pain, little gain

Nowhere is the "more is more" mentality as evident as in our approach to standardized exams. Indian education has this obsession for high-stakes examinations. Entire coaching industries thrive on the premise that students must ace these milestones at all costs. Parents double down on exam prep, schools teach to the test, and students internalize that their worth is tied to percentages and ranks.

Recent trends underscore how distorted this has become. In 2025, over two lakh CBSE students scored above 90% across classes 10 and 12. When one in ten or more examinees is getting 90% plus, it suggests either an incredible leap in learning or grade inflation. Education experts fear it's the latter. Chandrika Shenoy, an independent educator, emphasizes a deeper problem: "....board exams are still glorified. As long as the emphasis on exam performance persists, innovative approaches will struggle to take root, and the traditional pattern of education will continue unabated.” We need a massive mindset shift. The purpose of schooling should be to make kids lifelong learners – adaptable, curious, resilient. Right now, exams do the opposite.

The human cost is severe. India's National Crime Records Bureau found that 7.6% of all suicides in 2022 were committed by students, with exam failure cited as a key factor. All of this is clearly indicative of the failure of our obsession to get children to do more!

Voices from educators

Educators across the ecosystem point to the need for "less is more" to achieve deeper learning and student wellbeing. For that to happen, the system also needs to really reimagine what millions of our over-burdened teachers are doing today. Koushik Prabhakaran, a CSR leader, emphasizes reducing non-value-adding workload on teachers: "They are bogged down with election work, data reports, endless paperwork. Freeing up teachers' time is crucial so they can focus on improving their teaching."

Arya Mishra, a teacher trainer, advocates for small but transformative changes. She has seen how simply changing classroom seating from rigid rows to group clusters transforms energy and engagement. "The traditional, militaristic way of seating is limiting interaction," she notes.

Sandeep Kannikanti, founder of a private education company, wants to shift focus from curriculum and exams to children's curiosity: "….the future is uncertain, so why are we drilling kids on content that might be irrelevant? Let children's questions lead the way, not just textbooks. I wish every child woke up eager to go to school."

Arya V, a non-profit leader, reflects on her own experience: "Growing up, I never associated joy with learning – it was always exam-focused. In our program, we make classrooms more engaging through peer teaching. It's amazing how performance improves when kids are actually enjoying the process."

These voices speak to different facets of reform, but there is a common thread between all these voices. They all are advocating for less rigidity, less pressure, less top-down mandates. In their place, creativity, future-ready skills, joy, student agency is what is key. You can't have a playful, curiosity-driven classroom and simultaneously have kids preparing for 10 different exams.

Quality over quantity: the three Rs

So what fills the space when we begin to lighten the load? The answer, many educators agree, lies not in adding new material, but in shifting focus to what Kiran Bir Sethi, founder of Riverside School, calls the 3 Rs: Rigor, Relevance, and Relationships. It’s a simple yet powerful framework that speaks not just to what we teach, but how we create meaningful learning.

Relevance is about helping students see real-world value in what they learn. Much of today’s curriculum feels abstract or disconnected. But when lessons draw from students’ lives through local issues, hands-on work, or personal projects, engagement deepens. Rigor is not about adding more things to the plate but about going deeper. A focused syllabus often demands more thinking than a broad one. Let students build, question, and apply. And at the core of it all is relationships. Learning thrives when teachers know their students, when peers work together, and when schools are connected to their communities. But this kind of teaching needs space and time, both of which are lost in an overloaded system.

The way forward

Shifting to "less is more" requires action at multiple levels. At the policy level, governments need to periodically reimagine what children learn and boldly trim outdated content. Individual schools don't have to wait for systemic change. They can innovate with smaller shifts, as simple as a tweak in the timetable. Countries that consistently top education benchmarks, like Finland, often have shorter school days, less homework, and leaner curricula, yet achieve superior outcomes (Finland Toolbox, 2021). Finnish students spend less time in class than most of their peers, and the system emphasizes learning how to learn over how much to learn.

The notion that "less is more" in education is not a utopian idea. It is one of the most critical shifts that needs to happen in the education system today. Doing less structurally can create space for doing more substantively.

If we continue piling more on an already overloaded system, we risk breaking the spirit of learners and educators alike. But if we have the courage to lighten the load, we might ignite a love of learning that enables this generation to heights we haven't imagined.

References

  • Singh, Nandini. "India faces growing job crisis: Just 42.6% of graduates are employable." Business Standard.
  • ET Online. "PhD holders, graduates apply for peon jobs in India." The Economic Times.
  • ThePrint. "Millions apply for few jobs: Raghuram Rajan on PhDs taking peon roles, India’s employment crisis." ThePrint.
  • Press Information Bureau. "NEP 2020 emphasizes reducing curriculum content to promote critical thinking." PIB India.
  • The Indian Express. "School syllabus to be halved by 2019: Javadekar." The Indian Express.
  • Samath,Nirupa "NEP’s three-language formula a burden." DTNext.
  • Chakrabarty, Roshni. "CBSE 2025 results: Over 2 lakh students score above 90%." India Today.
  • Breen, Amanda. "This 15-year-old turned her side hustle into a $3 million business." Entrepreneur.
  • Watts, Jonathan "Greta Thunberg, schoolgirl climate change warrior: ‘Some people can let things go. I can’t.’" The Guardian.
  • Article-14. "Over 13,000 student suicides in 2021, many due to exam pressure." Article-14.com.

Vignesh Krishnan, Director of Global Expansion, Christel House International. Christel House is a global network of schools across five countries that delivers a comprehensive educational experience for children from socio-economically disadvantaged backgrounds. He can be reached at vignesh@christelhouse.org.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Vignesh Krishnan
Is “more” in education better – or is it breaking students and teachers alike? Vignesh Krishnan presents the case for deeper learning: through a lesser curriculum and fewer exams.

item.title: Connecting books to backyards
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Saidevi Sanjeeviraja

In real life, subjects do not exist in silos. A walk through any neighborhood proves this: you see how geography shapes a city’s biodiversity, how social structures affect waste management, how science explains why trees grow a certain way, or why a drain overflows during monsoon. But step inside a classroom, and these living connections are replaced with separate textbooks, separate periods, and separate exams.

If I could change education, I would begin by weaving subjects back together – right where students live and learn every day.

We know that systemic change takes time. The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 itself talks about breaking silos and integrating learning. But this vision cannot come alive through policy alone – it needs living examples that show how it works, even inside everyday schools.

At Thicket Tales, this is what we build through our flagship program, Learn Around Nature (LAN) – a year-long, experiential learning journey for middle school students, especially from low-income families. It weaves science, geography, and social science together through outdoor classrooms, experiments, discussions, and real projects.

Place-based education: breaking silos in three proven ways

Place-based education connects concepts and surroundings – and here are three ways we see this every day through LAN:

  1. Relevance – Students find their spark

When learning is rooted in real life, students care.

Today, many children lose interest in studies because they don’t see the point. They learn about biodiversity but rarely step into a park to spot a single bird. They memorize facts about water systems but don’t walk to the neighborhood drain to ask why it floods every monsoon.

LAN flips this. Textbooks become field guides. Schools become launch pads for outdoor classrooms.

Last year, 240 students explored how Bengaluru’s biodiversity and city design shape each other. Children, who once thought Nature was far away, discovered it right under their feet – in the cracks of sidewalks and tiny parks squeezed between buildings.

One 8th grader told us:

“Watching Nature is super cool! It helps us notice everything around us and makes us feel calm and happy.”


When learning connects to daily life, students don’t just remember facts – they begin to ask:

Who lives here? What does this tell me about my city? What can I do about it?

2. Application – Textbook concepts come alive

Place-based education makes ideas visible and usable.

School books often cover landscapes, rivers, water bodies, scarcity – but these stay abstract unless children see them where they live.

In LAN, students did not just read about rivers – they traced the rivers of Bengaluru on an interactive map made by Paani, a local water initiative. They mapped networks of rivers and lakes that sustain their city – many of which they had crossed daily without noticing.

In that moment, “water scarcity” was no longer a line in a chapter – it became the story of their own city. Drains, rivers, and floods became real.

When students apply textbook ideas in hyper local contexts, they gain knowledge they can use. These backyard connections help children truly see their surroundings – the first spark of change-making. They start to ask: “What can I do about this?”

3. 21st century skills – from knowing to doing

When students tackle local problems, they grow practical skills for life.

Learning becomes action when students pick a problem from their own backyard. LAN students work on issues like waste management, increasing green spaces, and improving mobility. They research, test solutions, talk to communities, and present their ideas at public showcases.

This builds skills we call “21st century” but are really timeless: curiosity, observation, critical thinking, teamwork, clear communication. Our assessments show real growth – students improved collaboration and communication from 30% to over 60% in just one year. Over 90% of visitors at our showcase rated student presentations as clear, confident, and creative.

When learning feels real, children naturally grow into the citizens we hope they will become: thoughtful observers, effective team players, and efficient problem-solvers.

All of this connects back to climate education

Too often, climate education stops at awareness. We tell children the planet is in crisis but don’t show them how to act. LAN bridges this gap by giving students safe, guided spaces to test solutions that matter – starting with their own neighborhoods.

Through these simple, local acts – tracing rivers, mapping waste, designing greener spaces – children practice what it means to be climate citizens. They see how daily life and Nature are intertwined. They see that change starts here.

Bringing schools, teachers, and families along

This change is possible only when schools, teachers, and families believe in the power of learning outdoors.

We know it’s not always easy. Teachers need training, schools need trust, and parents need to see that an outdoor classroom is not a picnic – it’s where deep, meaningful learning happens.

That’s why we work closely with educators through training and design support. We help schools see that NEP’s dream of experiential, integrated learning is not a distant future – it can happen now, one backyard at a time.

A call to every educator

If I could change education, I would start by asking every teacher:

What story does your neighborhood tell?

What problem can your students help solve – right now, right here? The answers are waiting just outside the classroom door.

If we want students who care, who notice, who question, who act – we must give them chances to do these things for real.

At Thicket Tales, we are only one small example. But when we connect books to backyards, we don’t just teach lessons – we raise active citizens, ready for the climate challenges and local issues of tomorrow.

Let’s build this together

To see how our students do this, visit our showcase: https://heyzine.com/flip-book/0727fadea6.html

Education cannot change overnight. But every teacher can open a door, every lesson can become an exploration, and every child can discover that learning is not a burden – it is a way to shape their world.

This Teacher’s Day, ask your students:

“What’s the first problem you’d like to solve in your backyard?”

The answers may surprise you. They may inspire you. And they may just show us the future we need.

Saidevi Sanjeeviraja is Founder and CEO, Thicket Tales, Bengaluru. She can be reached at saisanjeev02@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Saidevi Sanjeeviraja
What happens when textbooks step outside the classroom and into the backyard? Saidevi Sanjeeviraja shows how place-based education helps students see their surroundings with a different perspective.

item.title: Building an equitable society: starting with our classrooms
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Indumathi Sundararaman

To build a truly inclusive and equitable society, we must begin with our schools. I envision an education system where no student – regardless of gender – is left behind, where every child can access and participate in quality learning experiences. My work, designing a gender and STEM curriculum, training educators, conducting parental workshops, and researching gender in education across government schools in multiple states, has indicated that systemic change is possible – if we approach it with intention and persistence.

This article is divided into two parts. The first, shares my experiences of implementing gender education in schools and its impact. The second discusses the challenges we face and the systemic shifts needed to make gender equity a reality in education.

What research tells us

My findings (Sundararaman, 2020), along with other studies (Chanana, 2007; Bhog, Bharadwaj & Mullick, 2012; Indumathi, 2014; Manjrekar, 2020), highlight deep-rooted issues in our school systems:

  • School environments often mirror and reinforce existing gender norms.
  • Teachers may unconsciously bring biases into the classroom.
  • Textbooks still contain gendered language and lack diverse role models.
  • Students engage in peer discrimination, often using sexist language.
  • Parental influence reinforces gender stereotypes at home.

These patterns result in far-reaching consequences. Girls, in particular, are less likely to pursue higher education, and the gender gap only widens in fields like science, technology, and leadership. This gap does not begin in universities or workplaces – it starts much earlier, in schools. Over time, these early biases culminate in the under-representation of women in science, technology, entrepreneurship, and decision-making roles across sectors. This is not just an issue of access, but also of aspiration. Girls may internalize these biases, believing they are less suited for certain careers. Boys, too, grow up within limiting scripts of masculinity, unable to express vulnerability or choose non-traditional paths. Schools have the power to break this cycle – if equipped with the right tools and mindsets.

Policy support for gender equity

India’s education policy frameworks recognize these disparities. NCF 2005 emphasizes the importance of schools and classrooms being inclusive. NEP 2020 furthers this vision by advocating for gender equity, empowering female teachers, and establishing a gender inclusion fund to support girls and transgender students.

Integrating gender education into the school curriculum leads to meaningful outcomes:

  • Challenges and dismantles stereotypes.
  • Encourages inclusive and safe learning spaces.
  • Promotes critical thinking about power and hierarchy.
  • Fosters student agency in education and career decisions.
  • Enhances learning outcomes by ensuring equal access.
  • Shapes respectful and equitable relationships.
  • Reinforces positive social norms.

A ground-level view: gender and STEM education

Partnering with Quest Alliance, I had the opportunity to design a STEM for Girls curriculum and work with teachers, head teachers, and parents. We aimed to bridge the gender gap in STEM fields and empower girls to make informed career choices.

The curriculum addressed gender and sex, gender norms, stereotypes, rights, exploration of self, careers, and showcased inspiring women scientists, all through an intersectional lens.

At first, progress was slow. Many teachers were uncomfortable using words like "sex" in classrooms and hesitated to explore these themes. But through sustained training, things began to shift. We started sharing our personal experiences, which helped teachers open up; they began to reflect on their lives, and gradually felt comfortable discussing these ideas with students.

During the workshop, a female teacher shared: “We both work, but when I go home, I fetch water and serve a glass/bottle of water to my husband.”

This sparked a flood of personal stories. Another teacher added, “I work and earn, but my debit card and PIN are with my husband.”

Some male teachers questioned paid maternity leave or commented on their female colleagues’ attire. But with time, many began to reflect on their roles more critically.

One male teacher proudly shared, “I cook in the morning, pack the boxes for my children and wife, while my wife washes clothes or does other chores, and we leave home together at 8. I cannot call this help; this is my responsibility.”

A female teacher talked about her sister starting a successful home business and now teaching others – men and women alike – about financial planning.

As the workshops progressed, defensiveness gave way to genuine engagement. Teachers began acknowledging the stereotypes shaping their lives and expressed a willingness to challenge them. These conversations humanized the issue. Teachers no longer saw gender as an abstract topic, but something that affected their everyday lives, inside and outside the classroom. As teachers began transforming their thinking, the ripple effects extended to their students as well.

What changed in classrooms

In a recent impact study (Sundararaman, Thirumalai, and Nadaf, 2025), we observed promising signs of transformation:

In an Odisha school, a teacher described how a gender hackathon helped students recognize and challenge stereotypes. She noted:

“Boys have understood that girls have interesting ideas to solve a problem.”

In Karnataka, a teacher shared that she conducts group activities where boys and girls hold hands and participate equally in sweeping, decorating, and leadership tasks; in her words,

“Sweeping works now boys are doing using long brooms… leadership tasks are also designated for both girls and boys, serving food, both boys and girls do together, even decoration in the school, Rangoli, flower decoration, they do them together.” (p.26)

Teachers noticed that these conversations and activities extended beyond the classroom.

“The things we discuss in school reach home as well,” said one teacher, adding “Some students can voice their views and opinions at home and assert themselves with their parents.”

In parent workshops, some mothers, especially from urban areas, expressed commitment to supporting their daughters’ education and delaying marriage so their daughters could pursue careers of their choice.

These testimonies show that gender education has an impact on students’ lives.

Challenges and the road ahead

However, this kind of transformation does not happen overnight. It takes years, and is not without challenges.

Despite progress, some teachers continue to believe that gender bias does not exist in their schools. Many face difficulties implementing the curriculum, especially when trained teachers are transferred or leadership is unsupportive. There is also the issue of scale – how do we ensure continuity and expansion beyond the intervention classes? Sustainability remains a major concern for NGOs: How do we institutionalize this work?

Moreover, real systemic change also requires political will. Gender equity must become a priority at every level of governance and educational leadership. Without structural support and budgetary allocation, even the best-intentioned programs struggle to survive beyond the project cycle.

Institutionalizing gender education

Systemic support is critical. State governments and educational institutions must step in. The possible measures are

  • Curriculum reform: NGOs like Breakthrough have worked with states like Odisha and Punjab to embed gender equity into the core curriculum – stories, role models, and activities are now part of English and social science textbooks.
  • Teacher education: Gender training must be integrated into pre-service programs like D.Ed. and B.Ed. In-service programs can be offered as certified courses in collaboration with universities to provide deeper engagement.
  • Continuous support: One-off workshops are not enough. Ongoing mentorship, peer learning networks, and institutional structures involving DIETs, SCERTs, and resource persons at district and block levels are essential.
  • School leadership involvement: Head teachers must be active partners. Their support enables peer mentoring and whole-school change.
  • Community engagement: Beyond schools, the government should launch campaigns to challenge gender norms and engage parents and communities in the conversation.

We must also leverage technology to build digital communities of practice, share resources, and foster collaboration among educators across regions.

Change is possible – but only when gender education is not treated as a one-time intervention. It must become a sustained, collective effort embedded in our systems, our schools, and our mindsets. When teachers, parents, policymakers, and students all take ownership of equity, the ripple effects can transform not just classrooms, but society itself. Gender education is not just an add-on – it is the foundation of a just, inclusive, and progressive society.

References

  • Bhog, Bharadwaj & Mullick (2012). Forging a Vocabulary for the Nation: A Feminist Reading of Language Textbooks, Contemporary Education Dialogue, 9(1), pp. 39-61
  • Chanana, K. (2007). Globalisation, higher education and gender: Changing subject choices of Indian women students, Economic and Political Weekly. 42, pp. 590-598
  • Indumathi, S. (2014), Inclusion in Science Textbooks, Learning Curve, Oct, 2014, Bangalore: Azim Premji University
  • Manjrekar, N. (2020) Introduction. In N. Manjrekar (Ed.), Gender and Education in India: A reader (pp. 11-28). Delhi: Aakar publications
  • Sundararaman, I. (2020). ‘World would move ahead!’: exploring the learning of science and aspirations in the urban context through a case study. Cultural Studies of Science Education. doi.org/10.1007/s11422-020-09970-y
  • Sundararaman, I., Thirumalai, B., Nadaf, H. (2025). A qualitative study to evaluate Master Coach for Teachers (MCT) program. Quest Alliance

Dr. Indumathi. S is an educational consultant and researcher who has worked with various NGOs, including Quest Alliance and Breakthrough. She has developed and taught in various teacher education programs in universities such as TISS and APU. Her work and research interests span across gender and education, STEM education, and teacher professional development. She is based in Bengaluru and can be reached at mailindusow@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Dr. Indumathi Sundararaman
Gender bias in schools starts early. Indumathi Sundararaman writes about how classrooms can help students challenge stereotypes and pave the way for equity.

item.title: A teacher’s journey: breaking stereotypes, empowering students
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Moumi Sengupta

What does it really take to change how young people see the world – and themselves? Can it begin with the quiet courage of a teacher who dares to ask, “why not?” instead of waiting for policy level changes or reform in the education system?

When we talk about transformation in the education system, we often think about big reforms, changes in government rule, or policies, or systemic changes. But real change can start in small ways: a regular classroom, a simple question, or a teacher quietly determined to try something new.

I would like to share with you the story of Prashant Kumar Bhola, a teacher working in a government secondary school in a remote village of Puri in Odisha. This is a co-educational school, where most of the students come from marginalized communities and stay in hostels as their parents tend to migrate for livelihood opportunities. These students carry dreams along with pressure from their families, which is generally getting married early for the girls and start earning to support their families for the boys, and school is just a medium to get a certiﬁcate. In such an environment, how can a teacher single-handedly challenge deeply-rooted stereotypes and open new doors of possibilities?

Instead of restricting himself to teaching the students chapters of science in the classroom, Prashant sir chose to take a different approach to teaching. He first decided to bring about a shift in the mindset of his students. Prashant sir was aware that in order to do so, he would have to challenge stereotypes.

Challenging stereotypes – one act at a time

One day, Prashant sir walked into the classroom wearing nail polish. When he showed up with painted nails, there was absolute silence in the classroom, followed by curious stares and giggles. Instead of scolding the students, he calmly asked them a question:

“Why do you think nail polish is only for women? Who told us that? And does it matter?”

From there, a lively discussion blossomed. Boys and girls began to share what they have been always told like pink colours are for girls and blue for boys, how girls should focus on learning household chores so they can stay happily in their husband’s house while boys have to focus on their studies so they can start earning soon. The teacher gently guided them to see how these beliefs are deeply rooted in them, how they never felt like questioning such beliefs that limit their choices, creating unfairness and preventing them from expressing themselves.

Even small actions send a strong message; it’s important to question stereotypes, even if it is not allowed.

Prashant sir didn’t stop there. He continued to nurture the curiosity he had sparked, urging students to keep questioning their beliefs. In order to launch the Gender Ideathon-Hackathon (where students solve local gender-related problems with a tech-based solution), Prashant sir arrived to school riding a “pink ladies’ bicycle”. It became the talk of the school. Some students laughed, some were utterly shocked, but all were intrigued.

The goal was to challenge students’ ideas about colours, objects, or behaviours that are supposed to belong to one gender, and not to be funny. Through this action, Prashant sir wanted to show that men, women, and everyone beyond these binary labels should be free to express themselves, pursue their interests, and make choices without being pressurized by the society.

Talking about real-life gender roles

Education becomes powerful when it connects to students’ lived realities. In one of his sessions, Prashant sir began a conversation about how stereotypes inﬂuence education and career decisions, especially for girls. He asked the students:

“Do you think girls and boys get equal freedom in choosing their careers in your families? Are girls encouraged to become engineers, scientists, or police officers? Or do people say some jobs are not for them?”

Gradually, students shared their stories. Girls talked about being discouraged from pursuing science because it was considered “too difficult” or “boy’s subject”. Some said they were asked to focus on learning home management skills to get a “suitable groom”. The most girls were allowed was to become teachers because it would help them balance their family and work. Boys admitted that they were sometimes teased for liking art, music, or literature.

Witnessing his students open up, Prashant sir spoke candidly about his own experiences. He told them how he and his wife share all responsibilities at home – from earning money to cooking meals and managing household ﬁnances to chores even when relatives and family members question them – “Why do you cook dinner if your wife can do it?” I ask them, “Why not? We are partners. We help each other. That's how it should be.”

Such honest conversations made a deep impression on the students. They began to see gender roles as choices, not rigid rules. They started to question norms they might previously have accepted without thought.

Empowering students to become problem solvers

Rather than focusing only on having conversations, Prashant sir’s approach was about action.

He took these conversations beyond discussions and invited the students to think like innovators. Together, they explored different challenges that girls face in choosing careers, especially the lack of information and options and support available to them. Social norms, limited exposure, and a lack of career guidance led to this problem. Out of this brainstorming, came the idea of developing a mobile app to support students, especially girls, in finding careers – a digital tool that could offer timely guidance and information, and connect them with mentors. The students worked on designing the app by sketching the app’s pages, mapped out user journeys, and listed features they thought would be useful for them as users themselves, such as information about different job markets, professions, contact points for guidance counsellors, etc. This exercise was about a lesson in empathy, design thinking, and social change.

Through such projects, Prashant sir helped his students learn about solving real-life problems. His students started to see themselves as capable of creating change which has helped boost their conﬁdence.

Building a culture of conversation

The most profound impact of this teacher’s work is the culture he is building within the classroom.

Students are encouraged to question, debate, and share perspectives and personal stories. Discussions in the classroom are not dominated by the teacher’s voice.

This culture of respectful dialogue fosters trust and understanding. Students learn that it's a safe space to express themselves, admit their problems, and challenge each other’s ideas. These skills go far beyond school, they help create resilient communities when people come together to solve complex problems.

Change at school, change beyond

We often believe that change should be massive to be meaningful and impactful. But in this one school, small acts are planting seeds for systemic transformation:

  • Wearing nail polish by a male teacher sparked conversation about gender norms.
  • A male teacher riding a pink bicycle to school challenged stereotypes about masculinity.
  • Talking about personal experiences of sharing household responsibilities modelled equality in everyday life.
  • Identifying problems students faced to choose their career pathways and designing a mobile app gave students a vision of how technology can drive social change.
  • Encouraging open dialogue in the classroom transformed how students engage with each other and with learning.

These changes ripple outward. Students carry new ideas home and outside. They begin to ask their parents questions. Other students start showing interest. Other teachers become curious.

A small change that starts in a single classroom can spread across an entire school and perhaps, in time, into the wider education system.

A call to imagine

So, if you want to change something in education, start by imagining how it would look inside your own school. What would you like to see shift? Would it transform how things normally work? What new possibilities could it create for students?

Or think back: have you ever been part of a change in your school that altered the classroom environment or how the school functions? It might have been something as seemingly small as learning how to talk and listen to each other. Such shifts create deeper understanding, stronger relationships, and communities better equipped to tackle challenges together.

Or it might be a bigger systemic change – like transforming how children are assessed, or making sure every child, regardless of gender or background, feels seen, heard, and supported.

The story of this educator shows us that change does not always need grand programems or vast resources. Sometimes, it starts with a simple question:

“Why not?”

And sometimes, it starts with a teacher who dares to wear nail polish to school.

Before I ended my conversation with him, I asked Prashant sir if he ever felt worried about how people might judge him for doing all this – riding a pink bicycle, wearing nail polish, or talking so openly about gender and equity. He smiled and said:

“Of course, I get scared sometimes. But then I ask myself – ‘if I don’t do it, who will?’ Someone has to start. If I can help even one student think differently or feel more conﬁdent, isn't it worth it? And who knows, maybe one day, these students will become the teachers who change things even more than I ever could.”

So, I will leave you with this same question Prashant sir asks his students and himself every day –

Why not start the change with you?

Moumi Sengupta is a fun-loving facilitator who sees learning as a good conversation – deep, messy, and full of laughter. She designs learning experiences, writes reflections, and carries snacks (and stories). She believes real impact takes time, and shows up with patience and purpose. She can be reached at moumi@questalliance.net.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Moumi Sengupta
A male teacher in Odisha wears nail polish to class. What happens next helps students question and understand gender and identity.

item.title: Reimagining education for every learner
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Ranu Singh and Geetha R

On this Teacher's Day, as we honour educators who shape minds and futures, there's perhaps no better time to ask ourselves a fundamental question: If we could change education, what would it be? This isn't merely an academic exercise – it's an urgent call to reimagine how we nurture the next generation.

The answer, increasingly clear from classrooms across the country, points toward a revolutionary shift: moving from standardized, traditional uniform education to personalized learning that recognizes and celebrates each student's unique strengths, interests, and ways of understanding the world.

The limitations of standardization

Walk into most classrooms today, and you'll see a familiar scene: rows of desks facing the front, students of the same age working through identical worksheets, moving in lockstep toward the same assessments. This industrial model of education, designed over the past two centuries, treats children like products on an assembly line – expecting uniform outcomes from wonderfully diverse human beings.

The casualties of this approach are evident everywhere. The agile learner who can't sit still is labelled disruptive. The creative thinker who sees problems differently is marked as struggling. The students whose brilliance shines in unexpected ways never get the chance to discover their gifts. Meanwhile, even those who succeed in this system often graduate as skilled test-takers but lack the critical thinking, creativity, and collaboration skills our complex world demands.

A vision for transformation

Imagine instead walking into a classroom where learning is alive with possibility. Here, students don't just receive information – they actively construct understanding through exploration, questioning, and discovery. The teacher moves fluidly between roles: sometimes a guide pointing toward new territories of knowledge, sometimes a coach helping students develop skills, sometimes simply a fellow learner exploring alongside them.

In this transformed classroom, assessment isn't something that happens to students – it's something they participate in. Instead of high-stakes tests that measure memorization, students demonstrate their learning through portfolios that showcase growth over time, projects that solve real community problems, and presentations that allow them to share their unique insights and perspectives.

Perhaps, most importantly, this classroom buzzes with authentic communication. Students have learned not just to talk, but to truly listen to each other. They engage in meaningful dialogue, build on each other's ideas and navigate disagreements with respect and curiosity. This single change – learning to communicate effectively – creates ripple effects throughout the school community, fostering understanding, empathy, and the kind of collaborative problem-solving our world desperately needs.

Examples of change

This vision isn't a utopian fantasy – it's happening right now in some schools around the globe. At Sri Sarada Devi Vidya Kendra, Shivanahalli, a small village close to the Bannerghatta National Park near Bengaluru, students spend less time on standardized testing and more time on collaborative projects that integrate multiple subjects. Consistent efforts prove that personalized, student-centered approaches can deliver academic excellence. They don't just learn science – they conduct research that contributes to real scientific understanding. In our school, a science teacher along with the 9th and 10th grade students brought out a mini booklet to identify and document wild mushrooms on our campus.

Positive practical steps for individual educators

Every teacher has the power to begin this transformation, even within the existing constraints. The journey starts with small, intentional changes that prioritize students as whole human beings rather than as test scores.

Start with relationships. Before diving into the curriculum, invest time in truly knowing your students. What excites them? How do they learn best? What challenges do they face outside school? What are the social complexes at home? When students feel seen and valued, learning becomes possible in ways that surprise even veteran educators. Likith from 3rd grade was a brilliant boy. But, he had an irritating habit of coming up on his friends and beating them casually. His friends were not very appreciative of this habit. After a minor incident involving one of his friends, both Likith and his parents were given counselling sessions to try and rid him of his habit. Now in the 5th grade, he is the class leader.

Embrace student voice. Ask students what they're curious about. Let their questions drive some of your lesson planning. When learners have agency in their education, engagement soars. This doesn't mean abandoning standards – it means finding creative ways to meet those standards through student-driven exploration. Partha from the 8th grade was curious about flying kites. He designed his own kite and developed a flying technique that allowed him to fly his kite higher than the other kite flyers.

Diversify assessment. While you may still need to administer the required number of tests, look for additional ways in which students can demonstrate their learning. Can they create a video explanation? Build a model? Write a story? Lead a discussion? Interview their parents? The more pathways you provide, the more students can show what they truly understand. Thanushree from the 8th grade along with a small team of students made a documentary on saving Nature, thereby raising environmental consciousness on campus.

Focus on thinking, not just content. Instead of asking students to memorize facts, create an environment that will make them analyze, synthesize, and evaluate. Encourage them to ask good questions, not just find the right answers. These thinking skills will serve them long after they've forgotten the content. During a biology class, Saraswathi from the 6th grade asked about the creation and diversity of life, which led to a discussion on the various aspects of life, beauty of creation, colours, and classification.

Build a classroom community. Implement peer practices that will help students listen to and learn from each other. Morning meetings, peer feedback sessions, collaborative problem-solving activities and restorative circles all contribute to a culture where every voice matters. After an assessment in the 10th grade, peer correction helped students improve their writing skills.

Scaling systemic change

Individual classroom transformations are powerful, but lasting change requires systemic thinking. Schools and even boards ready to embrace this shift must consider several key elements.

Professional development needs to focus less on program implementation and more on pedagogical understanding. Teachers need opportunities to explore child development, learning theory, and culturally responsive practices. They need time to collaborate, observe each other, and reflect on their practice. This seems to be the first crucial step.

Assessment systems must evolve beyond standardized testing. While accountability remains important, we need measures that capture growth, creativity, critical thinking, and social-emotional development. Portfolios, performance assessments, and student self-reflection can provide richer pictures of learning than any single test.

School structures often need fundamental reorganization. Rigid bell schedules, age-based groupings, and subject-area silos can all work against personalized learning. Schools exploring block scheduling, multi-age classrooms, and interdisciplinary teaching often find that structural changes enable pedagogical innovation.

Community engagement becomes crucial as schools move away from familiar models. Parents and community members need to understand why these changes matter and how they benefit students. When families see their children becoming more confident, creative, and engaged learners, they become powerful advocates for continued transformation.

The ripple effects of change

When education truly transforms, the benefits extend far beyond individual classrooms. Students who learn to think critically become citizens who can navigate complex social issues. Those who experience authentic collaboration become leaders who can work across differences. Young people who see their unique gifts valued become adults who contribute their talents to society.

These changes also reshape the teaching profession itself. When teachers become learning facilitators rather than information deliverers, their work becomes more creative, more responsive, and more professionally satisfying. Many educators report renewed passion for teaching when they shift to student-centered approaches.

Perhaps most importantly, transformed education creates more resilient communities. When schools become places where every child's voice matters, where differences are celebrated rather than standardized away, and where learning is joyful rather than anxiety-provoking, they become models for the kind of inclusive, collaborative society we need.

A call to action

Many instances remind us that education's purpose isn't to sort students into predetermined categories, but to help each learner discover and develop their unique gifts.

The question isn't whether change is needed – it's whether we have the collective will to make it happen. In classrooms, schools, and systems, educators are already proving that transformation is possible. They're showing us that when we trust students as partners in learning, honour their diverse ways of knowing, and create communities of care and challenge, education becomes what it was always meant to be: “the manifestation of the perfection already in man”.Anever relevant astounding idea by Swami Vivekananda.

As we celebrate teachers today, let's also commit to supporting them as agents of change. Let's ask not just what students need to learn, but how they learn best. Let's measure success not just by test scores, but by the confidence, creativity, and compassion our graduates carry into the world.

The future of education isn't waiting for permission – it's happening right now in the hands of dedicated educators who believe every child deserves to be seen, valued, and challenged to reach their full potential. This Teacher's Day, let's honour that vision by making it a reality, one classroom, one school, one community at a time.

Ranu Singh is Principal and Geetha R is Co-principal at Sri Sarada Devi Vidya Kendra, Shivanahalli, Bengaluru. They can be reached at principal@ssvk.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Ranu Singh and Geetha R
Teachers don’t just follow lesson plans, they design how children learn. Ranu & Geetha write that recognizing teachers as designers is key to reimagining schools.

item.title: Experiential learning through MaaDoo – four practical shifts every school can make
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Team MaaDoo

If we could change schooling, we would replace “listen-and-recall” routines with experiences that let children do, ask, connect, and care. Many classrooms still divide subjects into silos, discourage questions, and judge success only by written tests. Project MaaDoo[1] (Kannada: “I do”) has shown in rural Bengaluru that four small redesigns; each feasible with existing resources, can turn any school into a laboratory of curiosity and community action. The activities below illustrate how learning-by-doing, inquiry, interdisciplinarity, and community engagement can coexist within a regular timetable, while quietly weaving in ethical values that exams seldom capture.

Activity 1: Inquiry box – normalizing student questions

Questioning is a powerful process in education. When students are encouraged to ask questions, they are required to think critically, which naturally fuels their curiosity and love for learning. Encouraging curiosity is the first step towards deeper understanding and meaningful learning.

Objective: Shift discussion from teacher-led recall to student-generated investigation, reinforcing research skills and confidence.

Steps

Mount a sealed “Question Box” in every classroom. Any language and any handwriting are welcome.

Example questions students have asked:

  • Why can we see our image in a mirror but not on a wall?
  • Do all objects reflect light?
  • Why don’t we see a rainbow every time it rains?
  • Why do plants take in oxygen at night?

These questions served as entry points into deeper inquiry with discussions varying from class to class. We kept the questions dynamic, building from one to another based on student responses, ensuring that everyone in the class was actively involved in the learning process. We also actively encourage students to ask questions related to the topic during and after the class.

Activity 2: Interdisciplinary lens – “inside an anthill”

When children first begin their schooling journey, they perceive the world as whole. They do not separate knowledge into compartments like science, math, or language. However, as educators, we often impose these artificial boundaries between subjects, leading students to see learning in fragments rather than as an interconnected experience. This fragmented approach can limit a child's ability to fully grasp and engage with the world around them.

Objective: Demonstrate how one natural structure embodies mathematics, physics, chemistry, and social science.

Steps

  1. Field sketch: Learners trace the termite mound dimensions, estimating volume with the formula for a frustum.
  2. Thermal probe: A kitchen thermometer records temperature at three different stages inside the mound; the data collected is taken back to class.
  3. Chemical test: Collect a soil pinch to test the pH; link acidity to termites’ calcium-rich saliva.
  4. Society simulation: Groups role play worker, soldier, and queen castes akin to the termite world to debate labour distribution.

In MaaDoo schools, test scores in biology and math improved together because pupils no longer saw formulas and food chains as unrelated. Observing termite cooperation prompts reflection on human social contacts.

Activity 3: Classroom to community – “rainfall in your backyard”

As facilitators, we often assume that we have taught everything necessary just because we’ve “covered” the textbook – which we treat as the ultimate teaching source. But in reality, learning has no boundaries. Traditional classroom teaching using blackboards helps to some extent, but it doesn’t completely fulfil the purpose of meaningful education. One powerful way to extend learning beyond the four walls of a classroom is by involving the community.

Objective: Connect meteorology to local farming decisions through real time data collection.

Steps

  1. DIY rain gauge: Using a half cut plastic bottle, students mark millimetre scales and calibrate with kitchen measures.
  2. Citizen science link: They call Varuna-Mithra, Karnataka’s rainfall hotline, to log the official village rainfall.
  3. Panchayat visit: Teams photograph the Atal Bhujal Yojana gauge, and interview officials and parents on water management.
  4. Data briefs: Weekly charts compare school readings with hotline data; anomalies trigger discussion on micro-climates.

Learners realize that “millimetres” on TV weather are actionable numbers for crop planning. Confidence grows when parents consult their children before sowing.

Activity 4: In-value-able: weaving values into everyday learning

Education is incomplete if it touches only the mind. Project MaaDoo therefore treats values not as an extra period but as the invisible thread that ties every activity to life. Activities are designed such that values are embedded within the experience, encouraging students to see the ethical and emotional dimensions of their learning. Each activity in the Maadoo project contains a core value and we design experiences that help students feel it:

Conclusions

Pramod, one of our class 9 students from Bannimukkodlu village on the fringes of the Bannerghatta National Park decided to take action after a classroom activity called ‘A day without plastic’. The activity explores the origin of plastic and its positive and negative impacts on living organisms, including humans. Moved by what he had learned, he gathered a group of his juniors and began collecting plastic trash from the edges of the forest near his village. The collected plastic was sent for recycling. Pramod’s initiative shows how experiential learning can inspire students to act beyond the classroom. When classroom knowledge/education connects directly with the real world, students not only learn – they lead.

The four shifts we outlined above are simple: a box, an anthill visit, a rain bottle, and an invaluable value. Yet, together they reinvent schooling experience. Knowledge moves from the blackboard to the village path; assessment moves from “write what I said” to “show me what you explored”; values move from the moral science period to lived practice. Recent research on inquiry and interdisciplinary learning underscores their efficacy, but Pramod’s cleanup or a farmer’s new sowing date are the metrics that matter most. This Teacher’s Day, we invite colleagues to try just one of the above before the end of term. Share your data sheets, sketches, and reflections in the staff room; let students present their findings in the assembly. The resulting buzz will be your proof that classrooms are too small for learning, and that children, when trusted, will exceed every curriculum standard we set.

Changing education does not always demand a lot of money or policy change. It begins when a teacher says, “Let’s find out,” steps outside, and watches curiosity light up a child’s face. What first step will you take?

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[1] An initiative of Ramakrishna Mission Shivanahalli supported by Youth for Seva and Texas Instruments

Team MaaDoo comprises

Mamatha R, Masters in commerce with a BEd, Mamatha is passionate about Nature and education. She has conducted nature awareness camps for government school children over the years and has been with Project MaaDoo since its inception.

Jaikumar R, a mechanical engineer with a keen passion for design, innovation, and nature, Jaikumar has also worked for many years to transform rural education. He has been with Project MaaDoo since its inception.

Nagesh OS, has a Masters in mathematics. He started out teaching engineering students, but his passion for Nature, education and serving rural communities brought him to MaaDoo. He now organizes nature awareness camps.

Nenike Hussain Basha Saab, has a Masters in physics. He is passionate about Kannada language and Nature. He has been working with Project MaaDoo for a few years now.

Team MaaDoo can be reached at maadoo.in@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Team MaaDoo
No marks or punishments, but projects and portfolios. Team MaaDoo shows what an alternative school that’s built around children looks like.

item.title: Compassion not competition at the heart of the education system
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Vikash Sharma and Ananya Pathak

The opportunity to write and reflect on what needs to change within the education system is both fascinating and challenging. It is challenging because to arrive at an answer, we have to inspect the suggestions, feedback, and ideas we received from hundreds of first-generation learners who enter the education system with a dream not only for themselves but often for their families and even communities, which have been deprived and humiliated for generations. Their individual education trajectories are seldom smooth, as such children do not come from an environment that nurtures learning, fosters creativity or cultivates their individual potential or uniqueness. As people working with such children, we understand the complex and multilayered issues within the education system from the perspective of the most downtrodden and disadvantaged groups.

The world of a first-generation learner

As first-generation learners from poor, economically disadvantaged, and socially marginalized communities, children come to the learning environment hungry, sleepy, exhausted (often due to extensive domestic/agricultural work to help their parents), and emotionally wounded. The challenge for the educator is therefore not only to deliver a lesson, help children decipher new ideas and concepts in a chapter, or formulate answers to the questions at the back of the lesson, but also to step into the child’s inner world, which is fragmented, broken, and wounded. As the founders of Shiksha Swaraj Creative Learning and Resource Centre, we have had the opportunity to work with children whose voices are seldom heard by the mainstream, but which are full of aspirations and desire to excel in their educational journeys despite the adverse conditions of life.

Any attempt to discuss the changes/transformations that the education system requires would be incomplete without listening to such voices. In the paragraphs that follow we will try to highlight some of the several aspects that are in desperate need of a metamorphosis within the learning machinery. These points have resulted from innumerable open-hearted discussions that we have been initiating at our centre. These discussions encouraged open dialogue, extensive questioning, and critical thinking about educational problems that these children experience on an everyday basis ranging from lack of basic school infrastructure, scarcity of teachers, and textbooks that do not speak to them to an often torturous examination system that has rote learning built into its very core.

Empathy and compassion: the key to a holistic education

For many children, the education system is no less than an alienating, gigantic, impersonal machinery that is oppressive with its emphasis on cut-throat competition, examinations that deepen hierarchy, textbooks that fail to capture one’s imagination, classroom interactions that are mechanical or lack the touch of empathy, etc. With a heavy backpack and droopy shoulders, many children dread school. Dalit children are often compelled to sit in specially demarcated spaces, drink only from designated taps within the school premises, and are mocked and ridiculed because of their caste. Female students assist with domestic chores like cooking, cleaning, attending to younger siblings at home before they attend school even as they constantly deal with the pressure of marriage as they approach adolescent years. Within many families in rural India, from an early age, young girls are oriented for marriage, and learning of household chores and nurturing tasks are given more importance than academic or other creative pursuits. Planning for a career is seldom a choice. Low family income, lack of exposure to the world outside accompanied by orthodox/conventional perceptions of gender, heavily limit the opportunities for most female students who struggle to even graduate from high school.

Working in rural Bihar has enabled us to see how the intersectionality of caste and class shapes access to education and opportunity for first-generation learners. The school, therefore, needs to undergo an urgent transformation and become a more holistic and liberating space, which not only imparts the curriculum and gives an officially recognized degree but also becomes a catalyst for social change. At the heart of such a transformation will be the need to work on an empathic and compassionate education that touches the inner world of the learners and aids in the birth of resilient communities that acknowledge shared issues and fight them collectively. For the school to become the center of social change, we need educators who have chosen to be in the vocation and are full of love and empathy.

School and the community

When we work with children from such challenging backgrounds we realize that our work as teachers is not just to teach mathematics or English, but also to understand where the learner is coming from – has she eaten at least one filling meal in the last few days, is the bleeding wound on his forehead a result of a domestic scuffle with his drunkard father, what is life like for the bright young Dalit girl who wants to become a doctor when she grows up, but her mother wants to marry her off the next month?

Our teachers, classrooms, textbooks, and examinations have failed to understand the world of these learners or make sense of their realities, and therefore they appear remote, unrelatable, and alienating. The illustrations in the textbooks are urban, the language is full of terminologies they have seldom heard, and most parts of the syllabus are beyond their understanding. With the fear of examinations hovering over them, most first-generation learners depend on poor quality guide books and guess papers, which they rote learn to sparingly pass their exams. Step into a rural government school in a state like Bihar and you will know how callous the system is. Yes, the leaking roofs of the classrooms after the torrential rains, blocked and overflowing drains inside school washrooms, absence of clean drinking water, contaminated school lunches, corporal punishments, cases of physical and mental abuse against children from the oppressed castes are only a few amid the innumerable challenges that children face on an everyday basis. Why would a child of an impressionable age want to attend school in these circumstances?

Other everyday pertinent challenges include the inability to understand the language of instruction, as in many rural areas students speak a different language making it difficult for them to understand the material or participate in classroom discussion effectively, unwillingness to spend on education especially in the case of the girl child, inadequate deployment of teachers or the appointment of only contractual teachers, complete lack of digital infrastructure and therefore a complete loss of touch with the rest of the world. Along with systemic alterations, there is a need to promote digital literacy so that the digital divide too can be bridged and children in rural areas are no longer isolated from their urban peers. It is ironic how in a globalized economy which boasts of enhanced digitalization, most schools in rural India have never even touched a computer, accessed the internet, or watched a film online. Computer laboratories do exist, but only in policy documents. No wonder, the wide and vast world that is connected and growing is far from the reaches of these children, keeping them literally confined to a dark box.

The school is therefore endowed with an immense responsibility to be the leading institution within the community. The school must be visualized not only as an institution like several others within the community but as the most important agent of social transformation. Whether it is communal violence, caste injustice, gender discrimination or any other social malice, the school must remain a sacred and sacrosanct institution where injustice is met with justice and all forms of violence are banished by unconditional love and empathy. This can happen if schools encourage members of the community including children, parents, and guardians along with other interested people to have an important say in the management of the school. This will help towards the much-needed decentralization of decision-making, incorporation of on-ground concerns and generation of authentic feedback for the future development and betterment of the school system.
