Other ideas towards the goal could include having regular community meetings and feedback sessions where all members of the community openly talk about educational challenges and possible solutions, contribute to its resources by donating funds, food grains, wood for making school furniture, or even their physical labor towards its maintenance from time to time. Such involvement may help build a strong sense of accountability and responsibility towards the school as a shared and important social institution. If the community participates in the sustenance and development of the school, the demand for quality schooling will also grow. All these efforts will certainly leave a positive impact on the functionality of schools and help to drastically transform people’s orientation towards education, especially in rural communities. After all, who can deny the fact that when the community at large is invited to become part of the school and people begin to see the light it can bring into their children’s lives, they will begin to give importance to education and thereby help to nurture the primary institution that imparts it.

It is important for us to acknowledge that there are innumerable challenges within the education system and it is impossible to highlight them all within the limited scope of an article. All we have tried to do in the above paragraphs is to share with you the realities inflicting the rural school system, especially for first-generation learners.

There is a need to address these issues and work on initiatives that will help transform the educational landscape, but this will require a strong administrative commitment, extensive community engagement, and the support of all the relevant stakeholders, without which it will all appear like a distant dream.

It is high time we all came together to make learning a pleasant and life-transforming journey for all children. Let this system not just remain limited to a degree providing machine but become a catalyst, a leader of social change. Let us unleash the powers of education as it frees us from the limitations of caste, class, colour, gender and all other limiting identities merging us all in the limitless oceans of a shared, common humanity.

(The article refers to Shiksha Swaraj Creative Learning and Resource Centre, Madhopur village, Patna. It is a pioneering creative learning and resource Centre started in 2021 dedicated to empowering rural children and youth through experiential, community-rooted education. Based in Bihar, it fosters critical thinking, self-resilience and local wisdom by combining academic learning with hands-on projects, cultural knowledge and sustainable living.)

Vikash Sharma and Ananya Pathak are founders of Shiksha Swaraj Creative Learning and Resource Centre, Patna, Bihar. They can be reached at vikashsharma946@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Vikash Sharma and Ananya Pathak
What if schools measured compassion instead of competition? Vikash and Ananya argue for classrooms rooted in safety, trust, and clarity.

item.title: Help every child shine
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neha Agarwal

As a teacher, I view education in schools as a dynamic journey of growth, not as a sprint to a test score. Classrooms should serve as launch pads where students begin exploring, driven by curiosity and supported by guidance. Lessons should be less about memorizing facts and more about igniting questions that deepen understanding and foster critical thinking.

Each student’s path is distinct. A child puzzled by algebraic equations today might excel at creative writing tomorrow. Errors should not be seen as failures but as opportunities that foster perseverance and critical thinking. As educators, we must provide the tools that will encourage students to overcome obstacles.

School should extend beyond academics, cultivating skills like teamwork in group tasks, compassion in debates, and innovation in projects. These experiences will mould well-rounded, resilient individuals. While grades provide benchmarks, the true aim should be to prepare students for lifelong learning. By nurturing a passion for discovery, we must empower students to embrace their ongoing journey, ready to adapt and thrive in an ever-changing world.

As a teacher, I believe school is about helping kids discover what they’re good at and love doing. Education isn’t just about fixing weaknesses – it should be about finding and helping each child’s strengths grow. Lea Waters, a renowned positive psychology expert and educator from Australia, says strengths have three parts: doing something well, feeling excited about it, and doing it often. For example, a child who loves building with blocks, makes amazing models, and builds every chance they get, has a strength. We need to find what makes our children light up. To focus on students’ strengths, we need to change how we think. I keep a journal to note down when my students’ eyes light up, what gives them joy, what they gravitate towards. I see that some of my students are great at solving problems, like figuring out a tricky puzzle, others shine in art or music, creating beautiful work, some love reading about animals, while some others are happiest playing basketball. Traits like being curious or caring also stand out, shaping how children act and learn.

When children do something they love, they often get into a “flow” – they’re so focused and happy that time just flies. This shows us that that particular activity or subject is an area of strength. For example, a student who spends hours drawing without getting bored is in a flow state.

Helping kids improve on their strengths happens in stages. First, we should spot the strength – something they’re good at, love, and do often. Next, we must help them practice it, like letting them perform in a school play. This takes effort from both the student and the teacher. Finally, the strength becomes something they’re good at, like starring in the play or winning a storytelling contest.

When we praise our students, we must be specific. Instead of saying, “I love how you made everyone laugh with your story,” we should say, “Your story had such great timing and expression – it made everyone laugh!” This helps students feel proud without feeling controlled.

Here are some simple steps to help children improve their strengths:

  1. Focus differently: Notice when children do well or seem happy, not just when they struggle. Be aware of your own habit of focusing on the negative.
  2. Give chances to shine: Let children try things they love, like joining a club or doing a project their way. A child who loves history might enjoy making a poster about it.
  3. Watch growth: Check if a strength is turning into something bigger. Does a child who loves drawing, start creating amazing artwork? Keep track of their progress.
  4. Encourage flow: Help children do activities where they lose track of time because they’re so happy, like building, writing, or playing.
  5. Focus on strengths: Talk about weaknesses, but spend more time celebrating what children do well to help them feel confident.

As teachers, we can transform our classrooms by focusing on students’ strengths. Start by observing your students with fresh eyes – notice when they’re excited or do something well, like leading a group or creating art. Write these moments in a journal to shift your focus from their weaknesses. Next, offer children opportunities to explore their passions, like joining a drama club or doing a science project. Watch how their strengths grow, from small skills to big achievements, and be specific in your praise, like saying, “Your painting shows amazing creativity!” This builds confidence without pressure. Encourage activities where children lose track of time, showing they’re using their strengths. Reflect on your own biases – do you focus too much on low grades? By celebrating strengths, we help children feel proud and motivated.

If we don’t, who will? Let’s commit to nurturing every child’s unique gifts, creating a classroom where they shine and grow into confident, capable people.

Neha Agarwal is a yoga teacher at The Peepal Grove School, Andhra Pradesh. She can be reached at lifeissimple.india@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neha Agarwal
While classrooms fuel competition among students, the comparison between them can diminish their potential. Neha Agarwal writes about how teachers can create spaces where every child feels seen and encouraged.

item.title: Through the green lens
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sakshi Uniyal

One of the most severe global crises is looming upon us – one that has already damaged the planet’s resources irreparably and has brought us on the verge of the sixth mass extinction. Climate change is no longer a concept limited to theoretical discussions in scientific circles; it has quietly permeated and altered every aspect of our existence. In the first 90 days of 2025, there were 87 days when some or the other part of our country experienced extreme weather including floods, storms, heatwaves, or landslides. Even as some states reel under the impact of severe floods, average temperature continues to soar to record highs across the country, and even states like Meghalaya are experiencing a major rainfall deficit. In this unpredictable scenario, children are disproportionately vulnerable to the severe impacts of climate change. This vulnerability partly arises from the crucial impact of the environment on their physical, emotional, and mental development and often manifests as climate anxiety in children, as several studies* have suggested.

In these troubled times, our only ray of hope is learning from the mistakes of our ancestors and passing down this wisdom to our children. Nurturing a generation of conscious and committed citizens can potentially drive positive environmental action from the grassroot level. Hence, I believe that integrating environment education into all aspects of classroom discourse is one of the most important changes that needs to be brought about in Indian classrooms.

The idea that inculcating environmental consciousness should form an integral aspect of formal education is not a new one. In 1991, troubled by the alarming rate of pollution in the Ganga, M.C. Mehta filed a PIL in the Supreme Court of India. This led to a landmark decision by the apex court – all formal education institutions, from schools to universities, were mandated to include a component of environment education. NCF 2005 advocated for a paradigm shift in education with environment education at the heart of this shift and indigenous knowledge about sustainable resource consumption as one of the guiding values. School textbooks developed with this view incorporated different aspects of environmental degradation, and discussions around pollution, waste management, deforestation, etc., became a part of science classrooms. These discussions brought theoretical concepts around global warming and climate change to the classroom. Students were exposed to the idea that human activities have caused irreversible changes to the planet, and these may be far more severe than previously thought. However, these discussions remained confined to the classroom due to gaps in the implementation of NCF recommendations. While theoretical knowledge was being imparted to children, scope for the active construction of knowledge remained missing in a majority of the classrooms. True environment education is not concerned only with knowledge – it encompasses a shift in attitudes and values, as well as the acquisition of skills necessary for such a shift. Its ultimate aim is to empower students to understand how climate change is impacting them, and search for measures to mitigate and effectively adapt to this change.

Such environment education cannot come through theoretical discussions and poring over textbooks alone. A sound theoretical foundation is undoubtedly necessary to initiate meaningful conversations. However, this knowledge should enable children to see the world through the lens of environmental conservation, instead of being an isolated discussion in science or social science classrooms. It should help them question activities they engage in on a daily basis, and then think of better alternatives. They should be able to see linkages between global catastrophes and the day-to-day problems in their community. Environment education should not stop at learning; it should progress to “learning and doing”.

A wonderful example of such an environment education programme is the Green Schools Programme (GSP) by Delhi-based think tank Centre for Science and Environment. Under GSP, students carry out a comprehensive environmental audit of their school campus. They understand how resource-efficient their school is by delving into its water consumption and conservation, waste management, green area, food habits, energy efficiency, and much more. They interact with members of the school as well as the local community and understand their challenges through the lens of environmental conservation. The audit is done every year, allowing students to see the cumulative impact of small changes year-on-year. The exercise seems daunting, but is broken down into sections, with each section having a set of small hands-on activities. When these activities and discussions enter the classroom, there is space for children to actively construct knowledge about sustainable resource consumption. Since this knowledge is embedded in their immediate context, it has the potential to cause a shift in how the students see the world. This is where they acquire a new lens, i.e., of environmental conservation, through which they can now analyze the world. Children who have had such experiences in their school are better equipped to carry them to their home and larger communities.

As part of the GSP team, I recently came across a commendable example of the transformative power of environment education. Students and teachers in Zila Parishad High School Gandaboyanapalli (Anamayya, Andhra Pradesh) managed to transform a half-acre barren area of land into a lush green forest using the Miyawaki forest method. Their most remarkable achievement, however, was not the transformation of the land itself – it was how the school brought together teachers, students, community members, forest department officials, and a local NGO for a common cause. The NGO provided financial aid, while the forest department helped with saplings suitable to the area. Community members also dedicated their time towards nurturing the plants. This joint effort beautifully illustrates the power that a dedicated community holds. The transformed land is now a classroom in itself – students gather there regularly to observe wildlife, water plants, and gather horticulture waste for composting. One can only imagine the insights that students might be taking away through such an experience.

Programmes like GSP highlight the scope of integration of environment education with existing curricula. Simple hands-on activities that push students to question their daily activities can be a part of all classrooms – be it mathematics or business studies. An honest effort on the teachers’ part can go a long way in ensuring such an integration. For instance, mensuration is a part of all grade 6 mathematics classrooms, where children learn different units and methods of measurement. Instead of learning about them theoretically, why not encourage children to measure the window-to-floor ratio of their classroom? The discussion can then take a brief detour into proper ventilation and air quality. Similarly, water wastage is a theme covered in most grade 7 science classrooms. The teacher can guide students to conduct a simple water audit of their school and detect leaks or other sources of wastage. Presenting such findings to the school administration would not only help manage the water resource better, but also build students’ conviction in their voice as changemakers.

As the climate crisis worsens, children cannot be passive observers of a jeopardized future. They must be active participants in the conversations around mitigating and responding to the climate crisis. Their voices must not only be heard, but valued and amplified. For this, the onus falls on our teachers and classrooms. The rote learning that has characterized our classrooms since colonial times needs to be replaced with a participatory classroom discourse that is rooted in a shared concern for the planet. Without the necessary skills to navigate a climate-risked world, we risk pushing our children into a world they can neither understand nor predict. Action-oriented climate education is our best bet to empower children to negotiate their existence on the rapidly warming planet.

* https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lanplh/article/PIIS2542-5196(21)00278-3/fulltext

  • https://www.nature.com/articles/s44271-024-00130-4
  • https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/00797308.2023.2287381

Sakshi Uniyal is an educator who has taught in a popular private school in Delhi as well as in a chain of central government schools. She specializes in science and inclusive education. She is currently working with the Environment Education Unit at Centre for Science and Environment, New Delhi. She can be reached at sakshiuniyal1993@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sakshi Uniyal
Climate education begins with everyday choices. Sakshi Uniyal writes about simple classroom practices that can grow into lifelong habits.

item.title: Rooted in the past, evolving for the future
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sreevidya M

School is a platform that shapes minds, teaches values, and builds the citizens of tomorrow. Education in India used to be more than just books and exams. It was about shaping character, building life skills, and nurturing values. Long before the modern classrooms and school bells, there was the gurukul system – a beautiful way of learning where students lived with their guru, learning not just lessons from the scriptures but also life lessons. This system emphasized holistic development, moral values, and practical skills alongside academic learning.

Today, while our school hours are structured between 8 am and 3 pm and the curriculum is tightly defined, the essence of the gurukul system still lingers. Several modern-day residential and alternative schools in India – like Rishi Valley School in Andhra Pradesh or Sahyadri School in Maharashtra – embrace holistic education grounded in community living and value-based learning. And this is an ideology that I believe all schools can follow – take the essence of the gurukul system and mould it to suit the modern times.

Role of institutions

I have noticed students feel disconnected from their school environment due to stress, peer pressure, and performance anxiety. This highlights the need for schools to go beyond academics and focus on inclusion, emotional support, and student wellbeing. With student-centred learning, access to learning support, inclusive learning practices, and active involvement of parents this can be achieved.

‘Children must be taught how to think, not what to think.’ – Margaret Mead

Lofty ideas of change might appear appealing, but profound impact comes from the smallest of actions.

The morning assembly constitutes the prayer, news, other presentations, and the national anthem. The significance of our culture should be reiterated in the assembly. Simple acts like greeting the teachers and thanking the service staff have to be encouraged. Students must keep their classrooms clean.

On the academic front, the choice of second language has led to a lot of debate among the parent community. Mother tongue has been made mandatory in many states. A large amount of money is spent on learning foreign languages, but there is reluctance to learn Indian languages. This should change. We should take pride in Indian languages.

‘A good teacher can inspire hope, ignite the imagination, and instil a love of learning.’ – Brad Henry

In a world driven by technology, where information is available in milliseconds, what makes the teacher irreplaceable?

It’s their human connection. Educators have a key role to play in the lives of their students. Even though content is available at the click of a button, the role of teachers cannot be negated. The methods of teaching could vary, but the desire is the same – overall development of the student. The class teacher can make an anecdotal record of each student, which gives an insight into the family background, hobbies, difficult subjects, or any other problems the student faces. Another area that demands attention is emotional safety. According to a 2022 India School Safety Report, nearly 50% of students across urban schools report experiencing bullying in some form. This reinforces the urgent need for schools to implement strong anti-bullying policies, backed by peer support groups and trained mental health counsellors.

As educators, if we can instil confidence and help our students overcome such challenges, it will reap wonderful results. Everyone wants to be heard; some are upfront, while others may need a push. As educators we have to keep a sharp eye on the behaviour of our students. Small steps can do wonders.

Apart from teaching, a teacher must inspire. They must open the minds of their students to dream big. They must expose students to the countless opportunities that await them. Teachers help students realize what their dreams and aspirations are and help them take the first steps in their careers. Real learning is fun, and this is what every teacher must promote. Students should be exposed to real-world applications of the concepts they learn, so that they appreciate what they learn and gain a sense of respect and admiration for the topics taught.

Teenage years are an especially turbulent phase of life. The teacher must try to understand their teenage students and what they are going through. They need to be the pillar that their students can fall back on.

The other challenge faced by educators is to engage the students actively. Teachers should be prepared with five-minute games like TCS – tap, clap, and snap, where students can follow instructions. Vocabulary games pique curiosity. They break the monotony of the class and act like brain teasers. Guess the word, give the antonym and synonym, word ladder, and fill in with vowels are some games that can be played in the classroom to engage students. Teaching a math concept on the football ground or through cooking and science through hands-on activities will spark student interest. In social studies, the exercise of the model United Nations will expose students to myriad issues around the world. Interaction with farmers and understanding the process of harvesting and how they manage the sales of products is a perfect way to connect with reality. Another learning strategy is cooperative learning, which involves group discussion, group projects, and dramatization. This facilitates sharing of ideas, teamwork, appreciation of members in a group, and acceptance of different perspectives. Education World India School Rankings 2023 highlighted that schools that adopted interdisciplinary and real-world application models showed a 15-20% higher student engagement rate compared to traditional rote-learning institutions.

Transformation doesn’t happen in a day. It is a gradual process. Tapping students’ potential is vital to boost their confidence. We must remember that every child is special and our job is to create an environment in which they will flourish. This is why we need to go back to how we started our learning journey; we need to find our way back to the gurukul system.

Sreevidya M is a high school facilitator at Sri Kumaran Children’s Home, Mallasandra, Bengaluru. She is a passionate educator whose greatest joy is watching her students grow in confidence, curiosity, and character. She believes in guiding children with patience and purpose. Sreevidya enjoys reading, music, poetry, and crochet. She can be reached at vidya.krish75@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sreevidya M
How can schools honour tradition while embracing change? Sreevidya M writes about how lessons from the past and present can evolve to meet children’s needs in the future.

item.title: It’s (not) just a piece of paper!
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Untextbooking and artifying learning resources

Tejas Joshi

Isn’t the act of opening or “unboxing” something – a gift, a box of sweets, a hamper, or a utility appliance – a memorable experience? Even in an overtly digital era, the eagerness and joy of receiving a new game box or simply opening a pack of crayons is unparalleled. As stretched as it may sound at first, I have embarked on a lofty mission to contrast this sensory, tactile experience with the pervasive “textbook” and the broader ambit of learning, in the hope of disrupting the presentation and delivery of curricula in educational discourse. The tool? The ubiquitous paper and a little creativity!

The textbook trap

As a student in a regular state board school, I, like many others, despised the drab, lacklustre textbooks that were the centrestage of every classroom, year after year. My interest in art and craft made matters worse, making the text-heavy design, basic illustrations, or cover art underwhelming in both appearance and appeal. They seemed rigid and uninspiring, yet authoritative!

Three aspects need reflection here. First, to recognize that the design and value of the textbook has been a matter of long-standing review. As succinctly put by Harrison et al (2022)1, (commercial) textbooks are “unable to be updated, reworded, or otherwise altered” by teachers. It is equally noteworthy that many teachers find themselves compelled to transmit the exact content and that it is hardly packaged in or accompanied by innovative pedagogical tools. Secondly, to quote Kimmons (2015)2, “Teachers must make their students malleable to the content rather than make the content malleable to their students.” These and several other limitations, in fact, fuelled the open educational resource (OER) movement, ideas such as of open pedagogy3 as also alternative learning avenues such as open courseware (MIT’s OCW4 being an eloquent example) since the 2000s. Thirdly, any pedagogical intervention, be it through an OER or teacher-introduced design, needs to present opportunities for learner-centric, modular, and customizable learning. For this to happen, the textbook must be deprioritized, or, in many a recent educational reform, become “untextbooked”5,6. The third aspect is especially important for STEM subjects, particularly environmental science, sustainability, and geography, which, despite their real-world interfaces and dynamically updating content, get trapped within the confines of a textbook, whose updation is time-taking and challenging. Clearly, their “packaging” begs more attention and innovation, if their learning is expected to translate into meaningful impact!

Purposeful paper

We often take the humble paper for granted, and understandably so! After all, from being a cornerstone in the history of humankind, paper has come a long way in occupying such a commonplace in our lives. The same paper that makes documents legal also helps us in our first steps of literacy – as somewhere to write on and read from. The paper that can take us around the world through colourful fold-out maps and newspapers is also the same material that our textbooks are printed on and firmly bound. Through an artistic lens, paper is the delight of the painter, printmaker, and crafter.

The reliance on paper in education also reflects a spectrum – from teacher handouts, notebooks, reference books, the notorious “test papers”, theses to craft projects involving paper, beautiful works of poster art adorning school walls, and much more. The tactile paper, despite stiff competition from the digital world and ideas of altogether digitized classrooms, seems like it’s still here to stay. Is there value in reimagining it for education, to reinstate a sense of wonder, curiosity, and stimulation? Can this very paper literally and metaphorically help “deconstruct” or “unbound” the textbook, and at the same time preserve a unique bridge between the digital and the analog (physical)? Finally, could the underrated paper inspire teachers to contemplate what they seek to teach and how they teach it in new, creative ways… perhaps complementing the textbook if not immediately doing away with it or going all-out digital? Four cases-in-point elucidate some approaches in this direction.

Not entirely a paper paradigm

Cards of various sorts illustrate an easy detour from the book format and are tangible for teachers and learners alike. Be it flashcards, information cards, playing cards on thematic subjects (often rooted in entertainment-based card games), activity cards, card prompts/quiz cards, or quick response (QR)-linked cards leading to further (digital) media/repositories, cards have offered myriad possibilities as pedagogical facilitators. Inherently modular, versatile, and at times messy, the sky is the limit in thinking of the pedagogic utility of cards whilst still relying on a palm-sized set of papers.

Worldwide, innumerable card-based resources on practically all subjects are on offer, even though they have not been as widely disseminated as their bound competitors. In India, except for a few publishers and outreach materials (e.g., field guides by the Bombay Natural History Society or my previous work on the chemical elements7), card-based tools are still considered niche and are plagued by high costs with low incentivization, mainstreaming, and limited design ingenuity. The important perspective here is whether we can move beyond perceiving this medium as a mere pedagogical aid and as a place for embedding current (updated) curricular content. A repository of visual, anecdotal module-based resources on relevant contextualized content, coupled with elaborate but flexible facilitatory handouts could open new ways of diversifying curricula through rich, co-curricular topics.

Infographic 1 provides a snapshot of two such projects spanning complexity and themes. Even the facilitatory activity handouts have great design potential, for instance collections from the successful Process Oriented Guided Inquiry Learning (POGIL) technique8 provide inspiration. Aside from their small footprint, cards and related formats such as posters and infographics starkly differ from the bound textbook being easily customizable and reprintable (with accessible web tools like Canva9 and Visme10), yet retaining their physical flexibility and form. With thoughtful design intervention, support, and impetus from national education providers, such cards can potentially constitute some of the accompanying “kickstarters” for a new term, year, or class.

Posing questions or imposing information

In the course of history, one of the greatest accomplishments with the advent of paper was its ability for record-keeping, documentation, and spreading information: functioning as a “proof” or authentic source of knowledge. For better and for worse, textbooks or conventional books continue to perpetuate this norm. Can they be perceived or suited as sites to ask questions or guide pursuits of learner- led discourses? To some proponents, this would almost imply challenging the identity and authority of the textbook. In one of our projects (Joshi, Wlotzka & Parchmann, 201911), we set out to redress this limitation for introducing the teaching of the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)12 by devising a super-compact range of “SDG Impulse Cards”. The resource, symbolized by the logo of a caterpillar, is visual, palpable and “directly speaks to the user” by posing questions rather than feeding ready-made knowledge – thereby allowing for an array of class or informal project opportunities.

Designed in the form of an accordion that folds into an almost finger-sized fan (Refer Infographic 2 and 4), each impulse card has a common anchoring framework and comprises questions or stimuli for users to independently engage with individual, local, and global considerations, and gradually learn science topics via competences and actions (solutions) towards ambitious goals. The material by itself may be diminutive, yet it straddles themes of global relevance and aims at ultimately leading to changemaking by stepping into the shoes of varied professions and getting a sense of what it takes to find answers to not-so-easy questions. Infographic 2 showcases an impulse card themed on slow fashion13.

Puzzling paper

If there is one art technique that does justice to paper, it would undoubtedly be the elegant origami. Whilst origami continues to be widely practiced within and outside education and its benefits are well-appreciated14, could it provide cues for the design of sophisticated paper-centred learning resources? Also worth considering is whether animations and scaffolding are only the forte of digital media like videos and interactive animation/web tools, or if we can exploit the versatility of the paper slightly further. A rendition of such a concept is the project “Chemtastic Biodiversity!”, a new learning resource to learn chemistry from, with and for biodiversity. Envisaged within the traditional Victorian fold technique, an intricate yet sturdy multi-fold mechanism that allows for an “unfurling” process akin to a puzzle, with strategically placed keywords, contexts, and guiding questions interspersing with visuals. Infographic 3 walks through the basic mechanism and visual affordances of its design; feel welcome to explore and interpret it for yourself!

Old and new

This article is a sheer attempt to reinterpret paper, not simply as a material but as a credible means to artify learning media for introducing real-world contexts/ topics in education without solely relying on curricular or textbook reform. Paper can be perceived as an authoritative tool for knowledge distribution – just like how it has been used – but also be creatively remodelled (albeit in small ways to begin with) for fostering uniquely immersive, memorable constructivist epistemologies. Could libraries of such materials, of course integrated with the rich affordances of the web, come together, and eventually decommission the textbook in the form of a composite “box of wonders” as an advent into the new year?

Such a concept has already gained promising traction with some of the above-outlined resources packed into the “Future Scientist’s Toolkit”15. Underpinned by ideas, questions, and possibilities towards realizing education for sustainable development, this toolkit made its way into schools, informal learning avenues, district supervisors, and teacher educators as part of a ministry-led pilot dissemination in the German state of Schleswig-Holstein (see Infographic 4). Can teachers and learners design and package their own bespoke kits, acquire and explore knowledge through these materials, and go on to exchange them widely as their own works of art?

In my humble perspective, the quintessential paper is at the confluence of human ingenuity, art, communication, and sustainability. Instead of heaping advert-laden lifestyle magazines, dusty answer books, or the umpteen wasteful ways in which paper products abound, a nifty learning resource may perhaps go a long way in posing big questions, triggering food-for-thought and finding answers to the great challenges of our time.

Within the realm of education and beyond, that piece of paper lying next to you has a lot more potential than in its present form. How would you like to reimagine it as a parent, teacher, learner, creator, or educator? Making a case for rethinking paper in a hyper-digitized world might seem retrograde to many, and whether such timid, pocket-sized resources can dethrone their contemporary – the textbook – is a bigger question. Even if some ideas and concepts in this piece appear fantastical at first glance, imagination knows no bounds!

References and resources:

  1. Research paper: Harrison, M., Paskevicius, M., Devries, I. & Morgan, T. (2022). Crowdsourcing the (Un)Textbook: Rethinking and Future Thinking the Role of the Textbook in Open Pedagogy. Open/Technology in Education, Society, and Scholarship Association Journal: 2022, Vol. 2(1) 1-17. https://doi.org/10.18357/otessaj.2022.2.1.30
  2. Research paper: Kimmons, R. (2015). OER Quality and Adaptation in K-12: Comparing Teacher Evaluations of Copyright-Restricted, Open, and Open/Adapted Textbooks. The International Review of Research in Open and Distributed Learning, 16(5). https://doi.org/10.19173/irrodl.v16i5.2341
  3. Introducer/ Book: The OER starter kit. By Elder, A. (2019). URL: https://iastate.pressbooks.pub/oerstarterkit/chapter/open-pedagogy/
  4. Learning resource: Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)’s OpenCourseWare (OCW). URL: https://ocw.mit.edu/about
  5. Project: “Untextbook” - as part of the Rethink Learning Design Project. URL: https://rethinkld.opened.ca/untextbook/
  6. Project/Podcast: “Untextbooked”. A student-created podcast-format project for history/world/journalism topics. URL: https://www.untextbooked.org/about
  7. Learning resource: “Let’s Meet the Chemical Elements!”. By Joshi, T. & Ladage, S.: HBCSE. URL: http://www.letsmeetthechemicalelements.org
  8. Project/ Resource: The POGIL (Process Oriented Guided Inquiry Learning) Project. URL: https://pogil.org/workshops-events/workshops/educators/resources
  9. Online tool: Flash-card maker-Canva. URL: https://www.canva.com/create/flashcards/
  10. Online tool: Flash-card maker-Visme. URL: https://www.visme.co/flashcard-maker/
  11. Article: Joshi, T., Wlotzka, P. & Parchmann, I. (2019). Eine nachhaltige Zukunft gestalten: BNE-Impulskarten für den Regelunterricht und für Projekte. Naturwissenschaften im Unterricht- Chemie, 172, S. 10-13. (English title: “Designing a sustainable future: ESD-Impulse cards for regular classes and projects). URL: https://www.friedrich-verlag.de/friedrich-plus/sekundarstufe/chemie/gesellschaft-nachhaltigkeit/eine-nachhaltige-zukunft-gestalten-3301
  12. Resource/Website: The United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). URL: https://sdgs.un.org/goals
  13. Article: Heinen, R., Joshi, T. & Wlotzka, P. (2021). Von Fast Fashion zu Slow Fashion: Kreative Zugänge zum Thema „Nachhaltigkeit und Mode“. Naturwissenschaften im Unterricht- Chemie, 184, S. 24-27. (English title: “From Fast Fashion to Slow Fashion: Creative approaches to the topic of sustainability and fashion”). URL: https://www.friedrich-verlag.de/friedrich-plus/sekundarstufe/chemie/gesellschaft-nachhaltigkeit/von-fast-fashion-zu-slow-fashion-9543
  14. Blog/ Article: Using Origami to Teach Children About Endangered Animals. By Chrosniack, J. (2021). URL: https://www.edutopia.org/article/using-origami-teach-children-about-endangered-animals/
  15. Learning resource: “Das Future Forscher*in Toolkit” (EN: The Future Scientist’s Toolkit). By Joshi, T. URL: https://learningamidstbeesandferns.org/

Tejas Joshi is an independent educationist and artist from Thane, India. He is the founder of the design studio Casa Eduökologic. Passionate about the confluence of education, science, and art, Tejas has studied and researched in India, the United Kingdom, and Germany. He can be reached at tejas@oldifluff.org.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Tejas Joshi
From flashcards and origami folds that carry questions and concepts, paper can become more than a bound textbook. Tejas Joshi writes about creative ways to “artify” the traditional textbook.

item.title: Steering a ‘U’ turn: how a Malayalam film inspired change in our classrooms
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Ardra Balachandran

For Malayalam cinema, the year 2024 was a gold mine. Kerala’s film industry catapulted to national attention when movies like Manjummel Boys, Aavesham, and Premalu achieved box office success across the country. Filmmakers were lauded for unique storylines and cost-effective production, and actors for their natural performances.

In the treasure trove of those Malayalam films, was another film, albeit a smaller one. The film was small only in terms of budget, star cast, and box office numbers; the film made it big when it came to intention and impact. That film was Sthanarthi Sreekuttan, director Vinesh Viswanath’s debut feature film, which was released in theatres in November last year.

The story is set in an upper primary school in Karettu, a village in the southern part of Kerala. Sthanarthi translates to candidate, and Sreekuttan is the name of one of the four protagonists of the film, all of whom are children. The title of the film refers to Sreekuttan’s candidature for the class leader post, and showcases his desperate strategies to make a winning attempt. Sreekuttan is a member of the back-bencher gang and a notorious latecomer, whereas his competitor, Ambadi, is a disciplined, studious boy who has a special place in the front row of the class and the class-teacher’s heart.

This film came to national attention after one shot from its climax made some teachers in Kerala rethink a tradition, leading to action, which resonated outside the borders of Kerala.

One visual, big impact

What is that climactic shot which effected a perspectival change? The traditional classroom system of arranging benches and desks in rows is shown to be overturned to make way for a U-shape, thus axing the hierarchy of students based on front-bencher / back-bencher labels.

During its theatrical release in November last year, the film didn’t get to ride the wave of appreciation. But once it was released on the streaming platform Saina Play, in late June 2025, it started making ripples. Albert Christin, one of the seven teachers at RCC L.P. School, East Mangad, in Kerala’s Thrissur district, was among the first who sat up and noticed. “He urged the rest of us to watch the film, and once we did, we were in agreement about implementing a change,” says Liji C.R., Albert’s colleague and the headmistress of the school.

They tried out the new system in class 1 and sensed an instant shift in children’s temperament. For small children, Liji says, proximity to their teacher is an emotional matter. Without benches, desks, and other students adding to the distance, the new system ensured that teachers were in close quarters with all students, equally. There were noticeably fewer tantrums. This inspired the teachers to adopt the system in the other six classrooms too.

Once RCC L.P. School posted visuals of the new layout of their classrooms on Instagram and made the film’s director a collaborator on the post, the idea garnered a lot more eyeballs. Many more schools in Kerala, like St. Mary’s English Medium School, Konni; St. Thomas L.P. School, Arthat; Pappinisseri West L.P. School, Kannur; Ramavilasom Vocational Higher Secondary School, Valakam; followed suit, all without any state directive. The wave soon crossed Kerala borders. On July 13 this year, A. Ragu Raman reported in the Times of India that the Tamil Nadu state education department has directed all its chief educational officers to pilot the system of arranging benches and desks in the shape of the Tamil letter ‘Pa’. Priyanjali Narayan wrote on India Today that this "quiet, but growing shift” is happening in states like Punjab and West Bengal too. Back in Kerala, the minister for education, V. Sivankutty set up an expert committee to look into a suitable model for the state’s education system, “one that may include eliminating the concept of backbenchers” – reported The News Minute.

What changes, really?

It is now roughly two months since this was implemented at RCC L.P. School, and Liji is elated about the impact. “Parents of today are much more protective and ambitious about their kids. Nobody wants their kids to be backbenchers in class. With this arrangement, parents couldn't be happier. For children too, the setting gives an equal opportunity, and it is especially beneficial for the reticent and the introverted. Of course, the mischievous ones can no longer get distracted or indulge in other businesses, because everyone is now in the ‘line of fire’ potentially. But isn’t that a good thing?” she asks buoyantly.

She adds that her school has students from economically backward communities. The students are smart and participate in classrooms actively, but most of them do not have a conducive learning atmosphere back home. This reflects in their performance in examinations, and the only way to resolve this problem is to give them the most attention in the school itself, in the most inclusive manner. There is no doubt that when children are seated in a semi-circular pattern, with the teacher standing in the centre, the class becomes more engaged with more scope of interaction between them. Not to forget, the teachers’ job has become much easier with this shift. Especially in lower primary classes where it has to be ensured that children write down everything, this system gives more visibility of not just the students, but their notebooks too.

But there is a subtler mindset shift involved in this whole revamp, an idea that is conveyed strongly in the film. The film’s protagonists are all children, as mentioned earlier; but the antagonist is a teacher. The character Chakrapani (called CP), brought to life by actor Aju Varghese, is everything that a teacher should not be: unscrupulous, arrogant, and megalomaniac. He favours the studious and smart Ambadi excessively, often berating other students in the process. There is one particular scene where he makes the back-bencher gang shift to the front bench, and then belittles them by asking them to move back after a public dressing-down. Stirring divisiveness by connecting students’ capacities and capabilities to where they are seated in the classroom is a theme that runs through the film, causing distress to the protagonists.

It is in this context, that the one fleeting visual at the end of the film, where the benches and desks are rearranged in a more inclusive shape, becomes powerful. In one stroke, there are no more back-benchers, front-benchers, or the hierarchy that comes with it.

The power of an idea

The director of the film Sthanarthi Sreekuttan says that this is not a new idea; it is common in Scandinavian countries like Norway and Finland. Vinesh himself sat in a semi-circular seating arrangement while he was a class 3 student in Govt. L.P. School, Pedikulam. It was the era of DPEP – the District Primary Education Programme – a national project launched in 1993 to achieve universalization of elementary education. He remembers the seating system as more engaging from a student perspective, but for various reasons, it did not last.

Vinesh, who is also one of the writers of the film, is jubilant that the film has sparked this conversation now. He says, “We received some flak from people saying the idea is not practical in classrooms with large student strength. We agree. But the point is that no classroom should have more than 30 students ideally, to ensure that all students receive the attention they deserve. There was also criticism that students should be able to see the blackboard in a straight line, and this seating arrangement will result in a sub-optimal angle of vision. But this is a perspective that comes from the thought that classroom teaching should be blackboard centred. It is high time we moved away from that. Also, it is possible to have place-rotation even in the semi-circular pattern. Despite these disagreements, we are overjoyed that our little film is the reason why this discussion ensued.”

On social media, it is not uncommon to see content around the nostalgia of having been back-benchers, how they are the ones who make it big later in life, etc. But Vinesh objects without hesitation: “No student who has been discriminated against for being a back-bencher will ever romanticize that idea. I was a back-bencher right up to college because of my height, and I have witnessed this discrimination first-hand. Being ignored isn’t an experience anybody deserves, let alone children.”

All of the situations and sub-plots visualized in the film have been derived from the personal experiences of the four writers: Murali Krishna, Anand Manmadhan (also an actor in the film), Kailash S. Bhavan (also the editor of the film) and Vinesh Viswanath (the director). Vinesh speaks on all their behalf: “All of us have had teachers with traits of CP in varying intensities. It is such toxicity that should be questioned. So, what really matters is not just the seating arrangement; it is the mindset around it, of the society at large and teachers in particular.”

There is no doubt that this small film has induced a silent movement, criticism notwithstanding. For the makers of the film, it was a sweet surprise that this idea was adopted voluntarily by various schools and the state too; it is not something they foresaw or even hoped for. If there is one takeaway for teachers from this, it is that small ripples too can bring about impactful change, and often, those start within us. U / C / V in English or Pa in Tamil – whatever name you call it, the one shape that truly matters is the shape of our thinking.

Ardra Balachandran is a media and communication professional from Kerala with a special interest in education and gender. She is an alumnus of the London School of Economics in strategic communications and can be reached at ardramaanasam@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Ardra Balachandran
Inspired by a Malayalam film, teachers across Kerala are making their classrooms more inclusive and engaging. Ardra Balachandran shares how rethinking the seating arrangement can reshape the classroom.
